Reflection:
This week's session focused specifically on oncology drug shortages — particularly the recurring shortages of cisplatin, carboplatin, fluorouracil, methotrexate, and vincristine that have disrupted curative-intent regimens in U.S. cancer care over the past decade. I learned that oncology shortages are uniquely consequential because chemotherapy regimens are protocol-driven, evidence-based, dose-and-schedule combinations, and any substitution or dose reduction has the potential to reduce cure rates in both solid tumors and hematologic malignancies. We reviewed NCCN's oncology drug shortage guidelines, which categorize substitutions by clinical equivalence and recommend specific decision pathways depending on whether the regimen is curative-intent or palliative. The class also walked through the 2019 vincristine shortage that forced pediatric oncology programs to substitute vinblastine in protocols where vincristine had been the standard for decades, despite differences in pharmacokinetics, neurotoxicity profiles, and supporting trial evidence. Oncology pharmacists at affected institutions became central decision-makers in regimen modification, working alongside oncologists, pharmacy leadership, and ethics committees.

This learning shifted my picture of pharmacy's role in oncology in a meaningful way. I had been thinking of oncology pharmacy primarily as a clinical and operational discipline — verification, compounding, counseling. The session reframed it as a discipline that, during shortage periods, sits at the intersection of clinical reasoning, regimen science, and difficult patient-allocation conversations. The personal piece for me was a sharper appreciation that the pharmacist's reasoning during a shortage is not abstract — it is the deciding factor in which patient receives which substitution and what the expected impact on their cancer outcome will be. That responsibility is significant, and it is part of why oncology pharmacy is one of the most demanding clinical practice areas in pharmacy.

Going forward, I will apply this learning by: (1) requesting an oncology pharmacy rotation during my advanced experiential year with a specific focus on shortage management, ideally at an institution that has navigated a recent major shortage; (2) reading NCCN's most recent shortage management guidance and ASCO's published statements on chemotherapy substitution during shortage so I am fluent in the published frameworks before I am responsible for applying them; and (3) shadowing at least one chemotherapy regimen modification meeting during my rotation where shortage drives the decision, so I see how the actual clinical reasoning is structured rather than relying on what I imagine it should look like.

This week we discussed oncology drug shortages, including how shortages of curative chemotherapy agents like cisplatin, carboplatin, methotrexate, and vincristine affect treatment outcomes. Substitutions can reduce cure rates and force protocol modifications. We also covered NCCN's published shortage management guidelines and the role of oncology pharmacists in deciding which patients receive substitutions. This is important to me because oncology shortages have direct outcome consequences in a way that few other shortages do — a patient who gets a substituted regimen may have a worse cancer outcome than they would have had with the original agent. At my future practice, I will be more aware of how oncology shortages move through the institution and how decisions are made. I plan to do an oncology rotation during my advanced experiential year and pay attention to shortage management workflows.
Cancer drugs sometimes run out and this is a problem. Oncology pharmacists have to figure out what to do. I will keep this in mind for my career.

This session focused on shortages of pediatric drug formulations — liquid suspensions, oral solutions, lower-strength tablets, and weight-appropriate concentrations — which are uniquely challenging because pediatric markets are too small to attract redundant manufacturers. I learned that pediatric oncology drugs (vincristine, actinomycin, cytarabine in some forms), pediatric antiretrovirals, pediatric antiepileptics, and pediatric formulations of common drugs like albuterol nebulizer solution have all been disproportionately affected by recent shortages. We reviewed how compounding from adult tablets is often the only workaround when a commercial pediatric suspension is unavailable, and how that workaround introduces its own safety risks: variable potency, palatability problems that affect adherence, beyond-use dating limitations, and the sterility considerations that apply if the compound must be sterile. The class also covered the role of the Children's Hospital Association and ASHP's pediatric pharmacy section in advocating for federal incentives that would reduce pediatric formulation shortage exposure, including pediatric exclusivity extensions tied to formulation availability.

What this clarified for me is that pediatric pharmacy practice has structural shortage exposure that adult pharmacy doesn't, and that the workarounds available are themselves clinically meaningful interventions rather than neutral substitutions. I had been thinking about shortages somewhat generically, assuming that pharmacist creativity and compounding could bridge most gaps. Today's session corrected that — for pediatric patients, every workaround imposes a different risk profile, and recognizing this is part of being a competent pediatric pharmacist rather than an enthusiastic one. The personal piece was understanding that as a future pharmacist, particularly if I work in any setting that serves pediatric patients (community pharmacy near a children's hospital, retail pharmacy with pediatric prescribers, hospital pharmacy with a pediatric service), I will need to be unusually proactive about shortage tracking and have substitute counseling ready before the shortage hits the dispensing window.

Going forward, I will apply this learning by: (1) bookmarking the ASHP Pediatric Pharmacy and the Children's Hospital Association pediatric drug shortage resources and reviewing them weekly during any rotation involving pediatric patients; (2) on any pediatric or pediatrics-adjacent rotation, asking my preceptor to walk me through the institution's pediatric compounding standard formulas for shortage-driven products and the specific BUD assignments and stability considerations behind each; and (3) committing to one detailed counseling session with a parent on a shortage-substituted pediatric medication during my training, so I have practiced the conversation about why the substitute is being used, what to watch for, and when to call before I am responsible for it independently.

Today we discussed pediatric formulation shortages. Pediatric markets are small, so there are fewer manufacturers and shortages happen more often. Compounding from adult tablets is sometimes the only workaround, but compounding has its own risks including variable potency and stability questions. The Children's Hospital Association and ASHP's pediatric section advocate for federal incentives. This matters to me because pediatric patients can't substitute as easily as adult patients. I will be aware of pediatric shortages on any rotation involving children.
Children's medications can be in shortage. Compounding is sometimes used. This is important for pediatric pharmacy.

This week's class focused on anesthesia drug shortages — propofol, succinylcholine, midazolam, fentanyl, and rocuronium — and their downstream impact on operating-room scheduling, ICU sedation, and emergency procedures. I learned that anesthesia shortages have been so persistent over the past decade that some institutions have developed pre-built tier substitution lists for every common agent, with second-line and third-line options pre-vetted by the anesthesia department and the pharmacy. The class walked through a case where a sustained propofol shortage forced an institution to use ketamine-based sedation in the ICU, and the institution only caught the increase in delirium incidence because the pharmacy team had built a balancing measure into the substitution policy from the start. We also discussed how anesthesia shortages disproportionately affect pediatric anesthesia (where dose-volume calculations are tight and substitutes may not have pediatric labeling), how the OR and pre-anesthesia testing communicate with the central pharmacy during ongoing shortages, and how scheduled vs urgent vs emergency procedures get prioritized when supply is constrained.

The personal piece for me was understanding how invisible the pharmacy team's shortage work is to surgeons and anesthesiologists in real time, and how much of the substitution policy depends on the pharmacy team identifying balancing-measure consequences before they become incidents. The propofol-to-ketamine delirium case was instructive — the substitution worked from a procurement standpoint, but the clinical balancing measure was what told the team they had a problem worth tracking. Without that, the institution would have absorbed weeks of additional ICU days and patient delirium without recognizing the pattern. I had not previously appreciated how much pharmacy's clinical credibility in the OR setting depends on this kind of upstream analytical work that the surgical team never sees directly.

Going forward, I will apply this learning by: (1) requesting a shadowing session with the OR pharmacist on my hospital rotation, with a specific ask to see the institution's anesthesia substitution policy and the most recent balancing-measure data; (2) reviewing the institution's published anesthesia shortage workflow if one exists, and identifying any agents currently in short supply where I might need to know the substitution pathway; and (3) tracking one substitution's balancing measures during my rotation — including any clinical metric the institution monitors for that substitute — so I can see the analytical structure rather than just the procurement decision.

Today's session covered anesthesia drug shortages including propofol, succinylcholine, and midazolam. These shortages affect operating-room scheduling and ICU sedation. Some hospitals have built pre-vetted substitution lists. We discussed a case where propofol substitution with ketamine increased delirium rates, which the pharmacy team caught only because they had built balancing measures. I will pay attention to anesthesia shortages on my hospital rotation.
Anesthesia drugs run out sometimes. This affects surgeries and the ICU. I will think about this.

This session covered vaccine shortages during pandemic response — drawing primarily on the COVID-19 vaccine rollout, the 2009 H1N1 vaccine constraints, and the recent RSV vaccine bottleneck for pediatric and older-adult populations. I learned that vaccine shortages differ structurally from drug shortages because vaccines are typically allocated through CDC's Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) priorities and federal allocation frameworks rather than through market mechanisms. This means pharmacy's role during pandemic vaccine constraint shifts from substitution and clinical reasoning toward allocation execution, recordkeeping, and equity attention. The class walked through how immunization-providing community pharmacies became central to the COVID-19 vaccine equity conversation in 2021, and how the federal allocation framework operationalized through state immunization registries, chain pharmacy partnerships, and the Federal Retail Pharmacy Program. We also discussed the operational reality of pandemic vaccine pharmacy work: scheduled appointments rather than walk-ins, specific cold-chain requirements (especially for early mRNA vaccines), strict documentation requirements for VAERS, and the public-facing communication challenges when supply is constrained but demand is high.

What this clarified for me is that pandemic vaccine allocation makes pharmacists into a public-health workforce in a way that even routine immunization practice doesn't fully capture. Routine immunization is delivered patient-by-patient based on individual eligibility; pandemic immunization is delivered against ACIP priorities under federal allocation, and the pharmacist becomes a node in an explicit public-health response. The personal piece for me was a clearer sense of professional responsibility — when the next pandemic arrives, the pharmacy I work in will participate in the public-health response whether or not the pharmacy has prepared for that role, and pharmacists who have done the preparatory work will be the ones who can execute well rather than improvise.

I will apply this learning by: (1) reading the CDC's COVID-19 vaccination program after-action review and the Federal Retail Pharmacy Program lessons-learned document so I understand what worked and what didn't from the most recent pandemic; (2) signing up for my state's pharmacy emergency-preparedness alerts and any immunization-program updates so I am notified about preparedness drills and pre-pandemic planning rather than discovering them mid-emergency; and (3) reviewing one ACIP vaccine priority document in detail to learn the allocation framework — the categorical priority groups, how they get translated into eligibility criteria, and how the criteria evolve as supply increases.

Today we covered vaccine shortages during pandemics, drawing on COVID-19, H1N1, and RSV examples. CDC ACIP priorities drive allocation rather than market mechanisms, and pharmacies played a major role in COVID vaccine distribution through the Federal Retail Pharmacy Program. Pandemic immunization shifts pharmacy into a public-health role with documentation, cold-chain, and equity considerations. This is important because the next pandemic will involve pharmacists similarly. I will pay attention to pandemic-readiness resources during my rotations.
Vaccines can run short during pandemics. Pharmacies help distribute them. I will remember this.

This session examined the policy debate around chronic shortages of generic injectable drugs in the U.S. — including proposals like Section 232 trade considerations for designated essential medicines, the Civica Rx nonprofit generic manufacturer model that emerged from a hospital-system consortium, federal proposals for direct investment in domestic injectable manufacturing capacity, and FDA's Quality Management Maturity rating program that aims to give purchasers visibility into manufacturer quality. I learned that the persistent shortage problem in generic injectables traces directly to specific market failures: thin profit margins (often single-digit or negative), heavy concentration of finished-product manufacturing among a small number of facilities, vulnerability of API supply chains to facilities concentrated in a handful of countries, and the absence of quality-based purchasing — historically, the lowest-price bid wins regardless of the manufacturer's quality history. Civica Rx was created to address this by offering hospital systems long-term, fixed-price contracts for shortage-prone generics with redundant manufacturing, with the explicit goal of restoring quality as a purchasing criterion. The class reviewed Civica Rx's published outcomes — measurable reductions in shortage exposure for participating institutions — alongside the limits of the model as a national solution.

What this was important for is the realization that durable shortage solutions are policy and procurement reforms, not pharmacy operational fixes. The personal piece was a clearer view of why ASHP and other professional organizations advocate at the policy level rather than only at the practice level — operational fixes inside any individual pharmacy can only do so much when the upstream market doesn't reward quality. I had been mentally categorizing pharmacy practice and pharmacy advocacy as separate activities; today's session made clear they are continuous, and that pharmacist credibility in shortage policy debates depends on both clinical experience and policy fluency.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading the most recent ASHP policy positions on generic injectable supply and the FDA's Quality Management Maturity rating proposal so I am fluent in the actual policy debate before I am asked about it; (2) following Civica Rx's published quarterly outcomes and learning what proportion of its target drug list has avoided national shortage during periods when non-Civica-supplied analogues did experience shortage; and (3) joining at least one pharmacy advocacy alert system (ASHP Advocacy, NCPA Advocacy, or my state pharmacy association) before graduation so I am positioned to respond to shortage-related federal regulatory comment periods when they open.

Today we discussed policy approaches to fixing the chronic generic injectable shortage problem, including Civica Rx (a nonprofit manufacturer started by hospital systems), federal proposals for domestic manufacturing investment, and the FDA's Quality Management Maturity rating program. The underlying problem is market failure — thin margins, concentrated manufacturing, and price-only purchasing without quality consideration. ASHP and other organizations advocate for these policy changes. I want to learn more about pharmacy advocacy and follow shortage policy throughout my career.
There are policy ideas to fix shortages. I should know about them.

Today's class examined international cooperation as a shortage response tool — including the FDA's increasing use of foreign-approved equivalents under the temporary importation program, the U.S.-Canada cross-border drug arrangements, and the broader question of regulatory equivalence between the FDA, EMA, and Health Canada. I learned that the EMA, Health Canada, the UK MHRA, and the FDA have functionally compatible quality standards for many drug categories, which makes regulatory bridging during a U.S. shortage feasible without compromising safety. The FDA's Importation Program identifies foreign-approved versions of a shortage drug, typically from EU regulators or Health Canada, and authorizes controlled importation through specific U.S. distributors who handle relabeling, recordkeeping, and distribution. We also discussed the political complications — manufacturer pricing differentials between countries, labeling differences (foreign products often carry only foreign-language patient information leaflets), unresolved liability questions if a quality issue is later identified, and the trade-policy implications of coordinated drug supply. The class also touched on the broader proposals for personal drug importation from Canada, which the FDA has historically resisted but which several states have explored under specific waiver programs.

What this changed for me is appreciating the FDA's discretionary tools as substantively powerful rather than merely symbolic. I had been treating FDA in static, rule-bound terms; this session made clear that during a real shortage, the agency has discretionary tools that can materially expand U.S. supply, and that pharmacists are the team that operationalizes those tools at the dispensing window. The personal piece was recognizing that the patient holding a Canadian-labeled or EU-labeled product at my counter is depending on my counseling for English-language information about administration, side effects, and follow-up — the foreign label is functionally unreadable to most U.S. patients, and pharmacist counseling becomes the actual information delivery.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing the FDA's Importation Program FAQs and the most recent list of authorized imported products so I recognize them when they appear at my counter; (2) building a personal pre-written counseling script for imported products that covers the foreign-label issue, English administration instructions, and a written takeaway summary I can give the patient; and (3) discussing one imported-product dispense with my preceptor during my retail rotation if one occurs, so I see the documentation and counseling workflow in practice before I encounter it independently.

Today we discussed international cooperation in shortage response. The FDA can import foreign-approved equivalents from the EMA or Health Canada under the temporary importation program. Imported products carry foreign labeling, which means the pharmacist's English-language counseling becomes the patient's main information source. This is interesting for my future practice. I will review the FDA Importation Program FAQs.
Sometimes the FDA imports drugs from other countries during shortages.

Today's class covered hospital stockpiling strategies as a shortage response — and the ethical and operational tensions they create at both the institutional and network level. I learned that institutions practice three patterns: routine safety-stock buildup (one to two months of inventory for high-risk drugs), threat-triggered stockpiling (in response to early shortage signals like FDA Form 483 inspection findings or manufacturer notifications), and strategic reserves for code/disaster scenarios. Each pattern carries real costs: capital tied up in inventory, expiration waste when stockpiled product reaches end-of-shelf-life before use, and — most consequentially — the systemic effect of one institution's stockpiling deepening the shortage for less-resourced institutions in the same region. ASHP guidance is to avoid hoarding behaviors and pursue regional cooperation through formal mutual-aid agreements, but the class acknowledged the operational pressure inside individual institutions to protect their own patients first. We discussed published examples of regional pharmacy networks that have implemented coordinated allocation during severe shortages, and the relative success of those agreements compared to ad-hoc institutional decisions.

What this clarified for me is that institutional shortage decisions are network-level decisions whose cumulative effect can produce or prevent shortages elsewhere. The personal piece is recognizing that 'doing what's best for our patients' is a defensible position within a single institution but can produce harm at the system level if every institution makes the same locally-optimal decision. This is one of the clearest examples I have encountered of an ethical tension in pharmacy practice that doesn't have a clean answer — institutions have real obligations to their own patients, and they also operate within a broader system whose function depends on cooperation. Holding both responsibilities at the same time is part of professional maturity.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) asking my hospital preceptor what the institution's stockpiling philosophy is — whether there is a written policy, what triggers stockpiling decisions, and whether the institution participates in any regional mutual-aid arrangement; (2) reading the ASHP and ISMP guidance on shortage purchasing ethics in full, including the case studies, so I have specific examples to draw on when I am asked about purchasing decisions during a shortage; and (3) when I am eventually involved in a stockpiling decision in my career, considering both the individual-institution and the network-level impact rather than defaulting to the easy local answer.

Today we covered hospital stockpiling strategies during shortages. Institutions buy extra inventory for safety stock or in response to early shortage signals, but stockpiling at one institution can deepen shortages at others. ASHP recommends regional cooperation through mutual-aid agreements. There are real ethical tensions between protecting your own patients and contributing to system-level shortage. I will pay attention to inventory practices and ask my preceptor about the institution's philosophy.
Hospitals stockpile drugs sometimes during shortages. This can cause problems.

This session focused on the role of manufacturer patient assistance programs (PAPs), 340B-related savings, foundation copay grants, and other access programs in helping patients reach shortage-affected medications. I learned that during shortages, prices spike for both the original product (when the limited supply is sold to gray-market intermediaries) and for substitutes (when demand surges across multiple alternative agents). Patients with high-deductible plans, coverage gaps, or no insurance often cannot afford the alternative — even when one is clinically available — which converts a shortage from a procurement problem into an access problem. PAPs and foundation grants become essential bridges for these patients, but the application processes are time-intensive and pharmacy-administered, requiring documentation of income, prior coverage attempts, and clinical justification. The class walked through how a hospital pharmacy team during a recent vincristine shortage worked through pediatric oncology PAPs to keep treatment continuous for several uninsured patients, with the pharmacy completing the applications themselves rather than relying on the manufacturer's process to do it. We also discussed NeedyMeds, RxAssist, and the Patient Advocate Foundation as core resources every pharmacist should be familiar with.

What this was important for is recognizing that shortage management has a financial-access layer that is functionally invisible until you encounter it at the dispensing counter. The personal piece is the realization that pharmacy team work to secure PAP coverage is among the most directly impactful patient-care activities I will do, even though it is bureaucratic and unglamorous. A successfully completed PAP application is the difference between a patient continuing their cancer treatment or stopping; that is real clinical impact, even though it doesn't look like clinical work in the traditional sense. I had been thinking about access programs as something separate from clinical practice; the class reframed them as an extension of clinical practice into the financial system that determines whether the clinical plan is actually executable.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) becoming familiar with NeedyMeds and RxAssist as core resources before graduation, and bookmarking the foundation databases relevant to my likely practice setting; (2) on my retail rotation, asking my preceptor to walk me through one PAP application end-to-end with a real patient (with appropriate consent), so I see what successful documentation looks like; and (3) keeping a personal reference list of foundation grants I encounter in my training so that, by the time I am practicing independently, I have a working knowledge of the access landscape rather than starting from scratch.

Today's session covered patient assistance programs during shortages. Manufacturer PAPs, foundation grants, and 340B savings help patients afford alternatives when their usual medication is in shortage. The application processes are pharmacy-administered and time-intensive. NeedyMeds and RxAssist are core resources. I will learn about these programs and shadow PAP applications during my retail rotation.
There are programs that help patients afford drugs. I should know about them.

Today we covered shortages of hazardous drugs — chemotherapeutics, immunosuppressants, hormonal agents on the NIOSH Hazardous Drug List, and certain antivirals — and the additional layer of complexity that USP <800> imposes on shortage workarounds for this category. I learned that compounding hazardous drugs from active pharmaceutical ingredient requires cleanroom access (containment primary engineering controls), validated personnel training including respirator fit testing and medical surveillance, and full PPE protocols throughout the compounding process. This means that most retail pharmacies cannot easily compound their way through a hazardous-drug shortage, and that the workaround capacity is concentrated in inpatient hospital pharmacies and specialty 503A and 503B compounding facilities. We discussed the case where a hospital used a 503B outsourcing facility for hazardous-drug coverage during a manufacturer shutdown and the documentation chain that supported FDA registration verification, USP <800>-compliant receipt, and patient-specific dispensing records. The class also walked through the categories of hazardous drugs most prone to shortage — the older-generation chemotherapeutics whose markets are too small to sustain redundant manufacturers, and the recent fluctuations in supply for newer biologics where a single manufacturing site issue cascades nationally.

What this was important for is recognizing that USP <800> structurally changes which institutions can bridge a hazardous-drug shortage, and that the answer is much narrower than for non-hazardous drugs. The personal piece is understanding that my future practice setting determines my role in this category — only inpatient and specialty oncology pharmacies can typically help, and the rest of the pharmacy ecosystem is functionally a bystander during these shortages. That awareness changes how I think about my career trajectory: if I want to be on the front lines of hazardous-drug shortage management, I need to be in a setting where USP <800> infrastructure exists.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing USP <800> shortage-handling provisions and the NIOSH Hazardous Drug List in full so I know what falls in this category and what doesn't; (2) shadowing a 503B receiving workflow on hospital rotation if available, including the verification of the supplying facility's FDA registration and USP <800>-compliant shipping documentation; and (3) understanding the limits of my practice setting before I face a hazardous-drug shortage so I am not improvising at the moment a patient asks me a question I am not equipped to answer.

Today we discussed hazardous drug shortages and how USP <800> limits which pharmacies can compound or substitute. Cleanroom access, validated training, and respirator fit-testing are required. 503B outsourcing facilities can fill gaps for hospitals. The retail setting generally cannot bridge these shortages. I will pay attention to USP <800> infrastructure in any setting I rotate through.
Hazardous drugs have extra rules. Shortages are harder for them.

Today's class connected drug shortages to supply chain integrity — specifically how desperate sourcing during shortages opens windows for counterfeit and substandard product to enter the U.S. supply. I learned that the World Health Organization estimates that one in ten medical products in low- and middle-income countries are substandard or falsified, and that the FDA periodically intercepts counterfeit shortage drugs at U.S. clinics that have sourced from unverified secondary distributors during desperate shortage moments. The 2012 counterfeit Avastin (bevacizumab) incident is the canonical example: counterfeit vials labeled as Avastin but containing no active ingredient reached oncology clinics that had been sourcing through gray-market channels because of a shortage of the legitimate product. We reviewed how DSCSA serialization, lot tracking, and verification — which we covered earlier in the regulatory module — are the structural defenses against this kind of shortage-driven counterfeiting, and how the pharmacist's verification of T3 documentation (Transaction Information, Transaction History, Transaction Statement) becomes especially consequential during shortages when the temptation to source from unfamiliar distributors is highest.

What this clarified for me is the direct link between shortage management and supply chain security — they are not separate domains. The personal piece is recognizing that the pharmacist verifying T3 documentation during a shortage is doing patient safety work that is functionally invisible until something goes wrong, at which point the documentation chain becomes the difference between an early-detected problem and a clinic full of patients receiving inert product. I had not previously appreciated how much DSCSA compliance during shortages depends on individual pharmacist diligence at moments when operational pressure makes corner-cutting tempting. That awareness reframes T3 verification as a high-leverage protective behavior rather than a paperwork formality.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) treating DSCSA verification more seriously during shortages, not less, and refusing to short-cut T3 review even when wholesaler back-orders make sourcing harder; (2) on rotation, asking my preceptor about how the institution verifies shortage-purchased product specifically — what additional checks happen for non-routine sources, who signs off, and what the documentation chain looks like; and (3) reading the FDA's Counterfeit Drug page and the published case studies of recent counterfeit incidents so I recognize warning signs in real time rather than after the fact.

Today we discussed how shortages can let counterfeit drugs into the U.S. supply chain. The 2012 Avastin counterfeit case is a known example. DSCSA serialization and T3 documentation are the structural defenses, but compliance depends on pharmacist diligence during shortages when sourcing pressure is high. I will pay attention to verification when sourcing during shortages.
Some drugs in shortage might be fake. This is dangerous.

This session examined how FDA Form 483 inspection findings at manufacturing facilities trigger and predict drug shortages, and how publicly available FDA inspection data has become a leading indicator that sophisticated pharmacy teams now use for shortage planning. I learned that a meaningful proportion of U.S. drug shortages stem from quality failures at concentrated manufacturing sites — once the FDA issues a Form 483 inspection observation, a warning letter, or an import alert, the affected facility may shut down for remediation, eliminating supply nationally if the facility is the dominant or sole producer of a particular drug. We reviewed how shortage tracking has come to incorporate predictive signals from public 483 findings, allowing some institutions to begin substitution planning weeks before the formal shortage is announced through ASHP or FDA channels. The class walked through the recent valsartan/losartan/irbesartan ARB shortages, where nitrosamine impurity findings at API facilities in 2018-2019 cascaded across the entire ARB class because the same API source served multiple finished-product manufacturers. The pharmacy teams that had monitored FDA inspection news got a several-week head start on substitution policy compared to teams that learned about the shortage from their wholesalers.

What this was important for is appreciating that shortage prediction is increasingly possible, and that pharmacy teams who watch FDA inspection data have real lead time advantages over teams that don't. The personal piece is committing to be the kind of pharmacist who watches upstream signals rather than reacting only to wholesaler back-orders. This is a practical, learnable habit that distinguishes proactive from reactive shortage management, and it requires nothing more than checking a few public FDA pages on a regular basis. The contrast between the institutions that had a substitution policy ready when valsartan was recalled and those that scrambled for two weeks afterward is, in retrospect, the difference between doing the inspection-monitoring homework and not doing it.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) signing up for FDA inspection observation alerts and the FDA's Drug Shortage Staff publications so the upstream signals reach me automatically rather than requiring me to remember to check; (2) following the ASHP shortage tracker and comparing its postings against the FDA inspection news so I learn how the upstream signals translate into formal shortage announcements; and (3) bringing FDA inspection signals into shortage discussions on rotation, even when the rest of the team is operating from wholesaler back-order data alone — the analytical contribution of looking upstream is exactly what a junior pharmacist can offer a more experienced team.

Today's class covered how FDA inspection findings — Form 483 observations, warning letters, and import alerts — predict drug shortages. The recent valsartan/losartan recalls came from nitrosamine findings at API facilities. Pharmacy teams that watch FDA inspection news get lead time on substitution planning. I will follow FDA inspection alerts and ASHP shortage publications.
The FDA inspects factories. Sometimes problems cause shortages.

Today's class focused on the critical difference between single-source and multi-source drugs, and how that distinction governs shortage risk in ways that the surface-level FDA approval data can obscure. I learned that even drugs with multiple FDA-approved finished-product manufacturers can still be effectively single-source because most of those manufacturers buy active pharmaceutical ingredient from the same upstream API facility — meaning a quality failure or supply interruption at a single API site cascades through every brand on the U.S. market simultaneously. We worked through several recent examples — heparin (where API contamination at a Chinese supplier in 2007-2008 affected multiple finished-product manufacturers), valsartan (where nitrosamine impurities at an API site in China affected the entire ARB class), and ranitidine (where NDMA contamination spanned multiple manufacturers using the same API source). The class emphasized that 'multiple manufacturers' on the FDA orange book provides false reassurance unless API sources are also diverse. Real shortage resilience requires diversity in API sourcing, not just diversity in finished-product manufacturing — a structural insight that has been built into recent ASHP and FDA shortage-prevention proposals but is not yet reflected in routine purchasing decisions.

What this clarified for me is that supply chain understanding requires looking past the surface label. The personal piece was sharpening my analytical instinct to look at API origin when assessing shortage risk for a drug class my institution depends on, rather than accepting 'multi-source' reassurance at face value. This is a kind of supply-chain literacy that pharmacists can develop deliberately, and it pays off both in routine shortage management and in helping leadership think about which drug classes are actually resilient versus which are nominally redundant. The valsartan case in particular was instructive — an entire drug class with seemingly diverse manufacturers was effectively single-source, and the consequence was simultaneous nationwide shortage of a drug class that millions of patients depend on for chronic hypertension and heart failure.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) learning how to read DSCSA T3 documentation to trace API origin, since this is the formal record that exposes the upstream supply chain; (2) asking my preceptor about API mapping for high-risk drugs at the institution — whether anyone has analyzed which drug classes are nominally multi-source but functionally single-source; and (3) being skeptical of 'multi-source' reassurance during shortage planning conversations and pushing for upstream API source verification before assuming a drug class is shortage-resilient.

Today we covered how having multiple finished-product manufacturers doesn't always protect against shortages, because many manufacturers share the same API source upstream. Heparin, valsartan, and ranitidine are examples where API-level contamination affected the entire class. Real resilience requires API-source diversity, not just brand diversity. I will think about API origin when assessing supply risk for high-volume drugs at my institution.
Drugs with multiple manufacturers can still have shortages. This is interesting.

Today's session looked at how lean inventory practices in healthcare — adopted from Toyota Production System principles in the 1990s and 2000s — have intensified shortage exposure across U.S. hospitals and pharmacies. I learned that the same just-in-time inventory principles that reduce capital tie-up and storage costs also remove the buffer that absorbs supply disruptions. The 2017 Hurricane Maria IV-fluid crisis illustrated this vividly: most U.S. hospitals held only a few days of saline inventory at the moment that Baxter's Puerto Rico manufacturing facility went down, which meant that within a week the national IV-fluid supply was constrained and within two weeks routine IV fluids had to be conserved through bedside protocols. Lean inventory had been an unambiguous virtue for years prior; in the moment of disruption, it became the proximate cause of patient-care impact. The class discussed the ongoing tension between operational efficiency and resilience and how some institutions have begun reintroducing strategic safety stock for high-risk drug categories — IV fluids, code-cart agents, antibiotics, and oncology essentials — even at the cost of inventory carrying expense and expiration waste.

What this was important for is seeing the operational tradeoff explicitly: inventory minimization saves money in stable supply environments and amplifies harm in unstable ones. The personal piece was a more nuanced view of inventory management as a clinical-risk decision rather than a purely operational one. Before today's class, I had been thinking of inventory minimization as inherently virtuous — clean, lean, efficient — without recognizing that the efficiency gain has a hidden cost paid in disruption resilience. The Maria case shows that resilience cost is not theoretical: when it cashes out, it cashes out as patients receiving suboptimal care because the system buffer was missing. Pharmacy directors who have been arguing for higher safety stock since 2017 are responding to a real lesson, even if the operational pressure to reduce inventory pushes back continuously.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) asking how the institution's inventory policy was adjusted post-2017 — whether safety stock targets were raised for IV fluids and other critical categories, and whether those changes have held over time or eroded under operational pressure; (2) thinking about safety stock as a clinical-risk decision rather than an operational one, and being prepared to articulate that frame when inventory cost discussions arise; and (3) bringing this perspective to any inventory-redesign conversations I am part of in my career, especially in institutions where operational leadership pushes for lean targets without considering the resilience tradeoff.

Today we covered how just-in-time inventory makes shortages worse. Hospitals had only days of saline inventory when Hurricane Maria disrupted Baxter's manufacturing in 2017, which created the national IV fluid shortage. Some hospitals are reintroducing safety stock for critical drug categories. This is a real tradeoff between operational efficiency and disruption resilience.
Lean inventory can be a problem during shortages. I will think about this.

This session examined how natural disasters — hurricanes, wildfires, earthquakes, tornadoes, and floods — generate sudden drug shortages by disrupting either local pharmacy operations or distant manufacturing infrastructure. I learned that the U.S. Strategic National Stockpile (SNS) holds emergency drug caches that can be deployed during disasters, but that activation is slow (typically requiring federal disaster declaration and ASPR coordination) and SNS coverage is narrow — primarily focused on chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear (CBRN) countermeasures rather than routine drugs. For routine medications during disaster response, the system depends on regional pharmacy mutual-aid agreements between hospital systems, chain-pharmacy emergency operations centers that reroute distribution from undamaged regions, and state pharmacy associations that coordinate emergency licensure and dispensing waivers across state lines. The class walked through how the COVID-19 pandemic accelerated several of these mechanisms, including emergency-credentialing programs that enable cross-state license use during declared emergencies and federal PREP Act protections that limit liability for pharmacists practicing under emergency conditions.

What this clarified for me is that disaster pharmacy is a discipline I had not previously recognized — and that preparation matters because deployment timelines during real emergencies are tight, sometimes hours rather than days. The personal piece is a clearer sense of the public-service dimension of pharmacy that I want to engage with deliberately rather than encounter unprepared. The pharmacists who deployed during Hurricane Maria, the COVID-19 vaccination rollout, and the Maui wildfires were able to be effective in those moments because their professional preparation included disaster-response thinking — emergency credentialing, mutual-aid relationships, situational awareness of supply chain vulnerabilities. That kind of preparation isn't part of every pharmacist's training, and pursuing it deliberately is one way I can be more useful in my career than the baseline.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading the ASPR's emergency pharmacy resources and the SNS deployment criteria so I know what is actually in the stockpile and what isn't; (2) investigating my state's pharmacy mutual-aid framework — whether the state pharmacy association has a formal MOU with neighboring states for emergency license reciprocity, and what triggers that agreement; and (3) considering emergency-credentialing programs (like the Medical Reserve Corps or state pharmacy emergency rosters) that would let me deploy if a regional emergency arose during my career.

Today we discussed disaster response and shortages. The Strategic National Stockpile holds emergency drugs but is mostly for CBRN countermeasures rather than routine medications. Mutual aid agreements between regions and chain-pharmacy emergency operations help with routine drugs. PREP Act protections and emergency credentialing enable cross-state practice during declared emergencies. I will think about disaster preparation in my career.
Disasters cause shortages. There are stockpiles but they are limited.

Today's class focused on how shortages of code-cart and emergency medications — epinephrine, atropine, sodium bicarbonate, naloxone, calcium chloride, magnesium sulfate — get managed in real time at the institutional level. I learned that ED and code-cart shortages have particular risk because the absence of a substitution decision-making window means staff may default to suboptimal alternatives in the moment, when seconds matter and there is no time to consult substitution guidance. ISMP recommends pre-published code-cart substitution protocols developed and approved before the shortage hits, mandatory in-service training when a substitute is added to the cart, and physical labeling that highlights the change so the difference is visible at speed during a code. We reviewed several real examples — recent epinephrine 1:1000 vs 1:10000 shortages that forced concentration changes within code carts, the 2018 sodium bicarbonate shortage that affected ACLS protocols, and the recurrent intermittent shortages of vasopressors used in shock states. The pharmacy team's role in code-cart shortage management is unusually visible: every cart change must be communicated, trained, and documented, and the consequence of a missed update is potentially a code-team mismedication during the highest-stakes clinical moment.

What this was important for is recognizing that shortage management for emergency drugs requires preparation before the event, not response during it. The personal piece is taking seriously that pharmacy work on code-cart preparation is invisible to the code team but determinative of what they have available. A pharmacist who has updated the cart, trained the unit, and made the substitution visible has done significant patient-safety work; a pharmacist who has skipped any of those steps has potentially set up a future code-team error. This is exactly the category of behind-the-scenes work that doesn't generate praise but does prevent harm.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) inspecting a code cart on hospital rotation with my preceptor, with specific attention to how substitutions from prior shortages are labeled, documented, and trained; (2) reviewing the institution's emergency-drug substitution protocols and any published ISMP code-cart guidance so I know the recommended structure; and (3) committing to in-service participation when emergency-drug substitutions are introduced during my training, even when attendance is voluntary, because the in-service moment is when the cart change becomes operationally real.

Today we covered code-cart shortage management. Emergency drugs need pre-planned substitutions because there is no time to think during a code. ISMP recommends pre-published substitution protocols, in-service training, and physical labeling of any cart changes. The pharmacy team prepares the cart but the code team uses it. I will be aware of code-cart inventory and inspect a cart on rotation.
Code carts can have shortages. This is dangerous in emergencies.

Today's final shortage session covered the role of patient advocacy in shortage policy reform — including how patient and family advocacy organizations have driven legislative and regulatory action that pharmacy professional organizations alone could not have moved. I learned that several recent FDA and Congressional shortage actions were driven by patient and family advocacy organizations such as the Pediatric Cancer Drug Shortage Coalition, the National Organization for Rare Disorders, the Cystic Fibrosis Foundation, and the Patient Advocate Foundation, who testified to Congress about the human consequences of shortages with specific patient stories. The pharmacy professional organizations — ASHP, NCPA, APhA, ASHP Foundation — have provided the clinical and supply-chain analysis that grounds those stories in policy options. The combined effect of patient stories plus pharmacy expertise has been measurable: provisions in the 2020 CARES Act that expanded FDA authority to require manufacturer reporting and increase transparency, new FDA Quality Management Maturity rating proposals, and several state-level shortage management requirements. We discussed how pharmacist participation in advocacy adds clinical credibility to patient stories, and how letters from pharmacy professional organizations to legislators carry weight precisely because they represent both clinical expertise and on-the-ground experience.

What this was important for is recognizing that pharmacy professional advocacy is part of clinical practice rather than separate from it. The personal piece was deciding to engage with advocacy work rather than wait for someone else to. I had been treating advocacy as something other people in pharmacy do, with my own contribution being limited to clinical practice. The class made clear that the policy environment within which my clinical practice operates is shaped by who participates in advocacy — and that pharmacists who don't participate are effectively letting non-pharmacists shape the policy environment for them. That framing changed my orientation: I want to be a pharmacist who participates rather than one who is acted upon.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) joining ASHP and at least one specialty pharmacy organization relevant to my practice setting before graduation, and following their advocacy alerts so I know when comment periods open; (2) participating in at least one Congressional comment period during my early career, even if my contribution is brief, so I have practiced the act of professional advocacy rather than imagined it; and (3) using patient stories — with appropriate consent and de-identification — to ground my advocacy in real harm rather than abstract policy arguments, because the combination of clinical expertise and patient narrative is what advocacy organizations have shown to be most effective.

Today's class covered patient advocacy in shortage policy reform. Patient and family advocacy organizations have driven recent FDA and Congressional shortage actions by testifying with specific patient stories. ASHP and other pharmacy organizations provide the clinical analysis. The combination has produced real policy change including provisions in the 2020 CARES Act. I want to be involved in advocacy and will join ASHP before graduation.
Patients and pharmacists advocate for shortage policy. This is good.

In today's session on insulin pen safety, I learned about the specific risk of pen sharing across patients in inpatient and long-term-care settings — a category of error that is structurally distinct from insulin dosing or concentration mistakes. The Institute for Safe Medication Practices and the CDC issued urgent guidance after multiple incidents in which insulin pens labeled for one patient were used on another, transmitting bloodborne pathogens (hepatitis B, hepatitis C, and theoretically HIV) via the pen mechanism even when needles were changed between uses. The class walked through the mechanism: even with a fresh needle, blood and tissue can be drawn back into the pen cartridge during injection, and that residual material then transfers to the next patient. The recommended safeguards include patient-specific labeling at dispense, single-patient pen use as institutional policy with no exceptions, removing multi-dose pens from automated dispensing cabinets that serve multiple patients, mandatory in-service training for nursing on pen use limits, and periodic audits of pen use across units. We also reviewed the distinct workflow for outpatient insulin pen dispensing, where each pen goes home with one patient and the cross-patient sharing risk does not arise.

What this was important for is appreciating that 'high-alert' applies to the device, not just the molecule — a high-alert classification can be triggered by the delivery system as well as by the active ingredient. The personal piece is recognizing that an insulin pen mishap is potentially a hepatitis-transmission event, not just a glucose-control event, and that the harm pathway is irreversible in a way that even a serious dosing error often is not. I had been thinking about insulin pen safety primarily in terms of dose accuracy and cartridge concentration; today's session reframed it as also a sterile-technique and equipment-handling issue with infectious-disease consequences.

Going forward, I will apply this learning by: (1) reviewing the institution's insulin pen policy on my first inpatient rotation, with specific attention to whether multi-dose pens are removed from shared automated cabinets and whether the patient-specific labeling workflow is operationally enforced or just nominally required; (2) confirming that any insulin pens I dispense are clearly patient-labeled with the patient's name, MRN, and admission identifier before they leave the pharmacy; and (3) raising any observed pen-sharing or ambiguous labeling immediately as a stop-the-line issue with my preceptor, rather than assuming someone else has noticed or that I should defer because I am a student.

Today's class covered insulin pen safety, specifically the risk of cross-patient pen sharing in inpatient and long-term-care settings. ISMP and CDC have issued guidance about this because pens can transmit bloodborne pathogens like hepatitis B and C even when needles are changed between patients, since blood and tissue can be drawn back into the pen cartridge during injection. The recommended safeguards are patient-specific labeling at dispense, single-patient pen use as institutional policy, removing multi-dose pens from shared automated dispensing cabinets, and in-service training for nursing. This is important to me because pen-sharing is a hepatitis transmission risk, not just a glucose-control risk, and the harm is essentially irreversible. I will check labeling and storage on my inpatient rotation and ask my preceptor about the institution's pen policy.
Insulin pens shouldn't be shared between patients because they can transmit infections. This is important to know in the hospital. I will be careful with insulin pens during my rotations.

Today we focused on the heparin concentration confusion that has been at the heart of multiple sentinel events in U.S. hospitals — most notoriously the 2007 Indianapolis incident in which premature infants received 1,000-fold heparin overdoses due to a mix-up between the 10 unit/mL flush vials and the 10,000 unit/mL therapeutic concentration vials, which had at the time strikingly similar packaging. The error killed three infants and led to fundamental changes in how heparin is packaged, stored, and administered. ISMP guidance now requires concentration differentiation through color-coding (different colors for flush, low-dose, and high-dose concentrations), separated storage with the high-concentration product physically isolated from the flush, removal of high-concentration heparin from neonatal floor stock entirely, standardized concentrations across the institution so different units are not stocking different strengths, and barcode scanning at the bedside as a final defense before administration. We also reviewed the FDA's subsequent labeling guidance for manufacturers and the role of professional organizations in advocating for these changes. The class emphasized that the most consequential medication errors often hinge on packaging and concentration choices made by the pharmacy team and the manufacturer, far upstream of the moment when the error reaches the patient.

What this was important for is recognizing that medication safety is, to a substantial degree, an engineering and design discipline — the safest version of a high-alert medication workflow has been designed so that the wrong action is harder to do than the right action. The personal piece is sharper attentiveness to packaging similarities I might otherwise overlook, and a more disciplined habit of treating concentration verification as a critical-review step rather than a check-the-box task. I had been thinking of medication safety primarily in terms of clinician vigilance; the heparin case reframed it for me as primarily a system-design problem in which clinician vigilance is one defense layer among several.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) learning the institution's heparin storage and labeling conventions on my hospital rotation, including which units stock which concentrations and where the physical separation is enforced; (2) flagging packaging similarities I notice — for heparin or any other drug — to my preceptor as potential safety contributions rather than complaints, since packaging similarity is exactly the kind of upstream issue pharmacy can address; and (3) treating concentration verification as a critical-review step on every heparin order I verify, not a default check, so the mental habit is built before any high-stakes moment arises.

Today we covered heparin concentration confusion. The 2007 Indianapolis case where premature infants received 1,000-fold heparin overdoses came from a mix-up between 10 unit/mL flush and 10,000 unit/mL therapeutic vials with similar packaging. ISMP now recommends color-coding, separated storage, removal of high-concentration heparin from neonatal floor stock, standardized concentrations, and barcode scanning at the bedside. This is important because medication safety often comes down to packaging and storage decisions made far upstream of administration. I will pay attention to heparin packaging and storage on rotation.
Heparin can be confused between concentrations. This has caused deaths in babies. I will be careful with heparin.

Today's class focused on magnesium sulfate as a high-alert medication in obstetric and critical care contexts — used for eclampsia seizure prevention, severe preeclampsia treatment, preterm-labor neuroprotection, and torsades de pointes management. I learned that magnesium toxicity (loss of deep tendon reflexes as the earliest sign, followed by respiratory depression, then cardiac conduction abnormalities, then asystole) can develop rapidly with administration errors, and that calcium gluconate must be immediately available as antidote on every magnesium drip. The therapeutic window is narrow enough that what looks like adequate dosing on a healthy patient can become toxic on a patient with renal impairment, and renal function changes during pregnancy can shift the magnesium clearance enough to push a stable patient into toxicity. ISMP guidance includes pre-mixed standard concentrations dispensed by pharmacy rather than manually mixed at the bedside, mandatory double-checks at infusion start, dedicated tubing labeled MAGNESIUM in large print to prevent line confusion (especially when the patient has multiple infusions running), structured monitoring of deep tendon reflexes by nursing throughout the infusion, and explicit calcium gluconate availability protocols. We also discussed why obstetric units have historically been HAM-dense environments distinct from general adult care and why pharmacy involvement in OB safety has accelerated in recent years.

What this was important for is recognizing that obstetric units are HAM-dense in ways that I had not previously appreciated — they have multiple high-alert medications running simultaneously on the same patient, with rapid clinical change requiring titration, and with two patients (mother and fetus) whose interests sometimes diverge. The personal piece is appreciating that pharmacist clinical leadership in OB requires earning credibility with a unit that has historically operated relatively independently from pharmacy, and that the credibility comes from being fluent in the unit's protocols rather than from generic clinical knowledge.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) requesting an obstetric pharmacy rotation if my program permits, with a specific focus on magnesium and oxytocin protocols, the pharmacist's role in OB safety committees, and the cross-discipline communication patterns; (2) reviewing magnesium administration protocols on any unit that uses them, with attention to monitoring frequency, antidote availability, and the documented response to toxicity signs; and (3) confirming antidote availability is part of any magnesium initiation I observe, even when the unit's culture treats this as routine — the verification is what keeps the antidote available rather than the assumption that it always is.

Today's class covered magnesium sulfate safety in obstetric and critical care contexts. Magnesium toxicity can be rapid — loss of deep tendon reflexes is the earliest sign, followed by respiratory depression and cardiac effects. Calcium gluconate is the antidote and must be available at the bedside. ISMP recommends pre-mixed concentrations, dedicated MAGNESIUM-labeled tubing, double-checks at infusion start, and structured nursing monitoring. The therapeutic window is narrow and renal function changes during pregnancy can shift magnesium clearance. I will be aware of magnesium safety in OB rotations.
Magnesium is used in pregnant patients. It can be dangerous if too much is given. Calcium gluconate is the antidote.

Today's session focused on oxytocin as one of the most-implicated medications in obstetric malpractice litigation, due to the narrow titration window between effective labor augmentation and uterine hyperstimulation that endangers the fetus. I learned that ACOG and ISMP guidance includes standardized oxytocin protocols with specified starting doses and incremental titration intervals, escalation criteria tied to fetal heart rate monitoring, and required pause-or-reduction triggers when contractions exceed defined frequency thresholds (typically more than five contractions in ten minutes, or sustained hypertonic contractions). The class walked through how the pharmacy team contributes to oxytocin safety even when the day-to-day administration is nursing-driven: standardizing the institutional concentration so every bag contains the same dilution, dispensing patient-specific bags rather than allowing manual mixing at the bedside, supporting nursing protocols and order sets through the EHR, and participating in OB safety committee work that maintains and updates the institutional oxytocin protocol. We also discussed the broader pattern that the safest obstetric units operate with explicit checklists and structured communication, not with informal experience-based judgment, and that the shift to checklist-based OB practice is one of the success stories of obstetric patient safety.

What this was important for is appreciating how HAMs in specialty units depend on cross-discipline protocols where pharmacy is one of several owners, not the primary owner. Pharmacy's contribution is upstream and structural, but the day-to-day administration safety depends on nursing protocol fidelity. The personal piece is recognizing that pharmacy clinical leadership in OB requires earning credibility with a unit that has historically operated relatively independently from pharmacy, and that the credibility comes from being fluent in fetal monitoring vocabulary, ACOG guidelines, and the unit's specific protocols rather than from generic pharmacology.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading the institution's oxytocin protocol if accessible during my rotations, with attention to the titration intervals, hyperstimulation criteria, and the documentation requirements; (2) attending an OB rounds session if my rotation permits, even just as an observer, so I see the cross-discipline communication patterns and can speak the unit's vocabulary in future encounters; and (3) understanding the fetal-monitoring escalation criteria — Category I, II, and III tracings — so that when I encounter an oxytocin order modification driven by fetal monitoring concerns, I can engage with the clinical reasoning rather than just verifying the dose.

Today's class covered oxytocin safety in labor. Hyperstimulation can harm the fetus, and the titration window between effective augmentation and danger is narrow. ACOG and ISMP recommend standardized protocols with specified starting doses, titration intervals, and pause-or-reduction triggers based on contraction frequency and fetal heart rate. Pharmacy contributes by standardizing concentrations, dispensing patient-specific bags, and supporting EHR order sets. I will pay attention to OB protocols on rotations.
Oxytocin is used to start or speed up labor. It can be dangerous if too much is given. There are protocols.

Today's class focused on the structured chemotherapy double-check workflow — typically a triple-check involving the prescribing oncologist, the verifying pharmacist, and the administering nurse, each independently verifying the regimen, dose, body surface area calculation, route, schedule, and supportive care orders. I learned that ASHP and ONS guidelines specify the elements of an independent check (no priming between checkers, separate dose calculation, documented sign-off, and explicit verification of regimen against the institutional standard) and that even a single skipped element is associated with harm in published case reports. The class also covered the role of computerized provider order entry with embedded chemotherapy regimens — the most successful chemo safety programs have built order sets that constrain prescribing to validated regimens with embedded dose calculations, supportive care defaults, and laboratory prerequisites. Most chemotherapy errors that reach the patient occur outside or in spite of these standard protocols, often when verbal orders, one-off regimen modifications, or 'just this once' workarounds bypass the structured workflow. We discussed several published case reports of fatal chemotherapy overdoses where the proximate cause was a deviation from the structured check process — typically a senior clinician's request to expedite verification that crowded out the independent BSA calculation.

What this clarified for me is that chemotherapy safety depends on adherence to a workflow that exists precisely to prevent the high-stakes, irreversible errors that this drug class can produce. The personal piece is preparing myself to push back when a workflow shortcut is proposed, even when the request comes from a senior physician or experienced pharmacist. The asymmetry of consequences is what justifies the discipline — the cost of an extra five minutes per verification is small; the cost of a fatal chemotherapy overdose is unbearable, and the shortcut is the most common contributor to that error pathway.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) shadowing an oncology pharmacist on a verification shift during my advanced rotations, with specific attention to how the independent check is operationalized and what the documentation chain looks like; (2) practicing an independent BSA check before each chemotherapy verification on rotation, using the institutional formula and double-checking against an independent reference, even for regimens I have seen many times; and (3) committing to never accept a verbal chemotherapy order modification without re-running the full workflow — the verbal modification is exactly the case the workflow is designed to catch.

Today we covered chemotherapy double-checks. Multiple independent checks by the prescribing oncologist, verifying pharmacist, and administering nurse are required, and ASHP and ONS specify the elements of an independent check including no priming between checkers and separate dose calculation. Most chemo errors that reach patients happen when the workflow is bypassed by verbal orders or one-off modifications. I will follow chemo verification protocols and shadow an oncology pharmacist during my rotation.
Chemo needs multiple checks for safety. The oncologist, pharmacist, and nurse all verify it.

Today's session focused on errors in total parenteral nutrition compounding — historically a high-harm category because TPN combines many components (dextrose, amino acids, lipids, electrolytes, trace elements, multivitamins, and patient-specific additives like insulin, H2 blockers, or heparin) that can interact, precipitate, or be miscalculated. I learned about the 1994 incident at a Michigan hospital where TPN with dramatically excess phosphate, the result of a manual compounding miscalculation, led to multiple infant deaths; this event accelerated industry-wide adoption of automated compounding devices, standardized institutional formulas, and structured verification workflows that we now treat as baseline practice. ASPEN guidelines now favor standardized commercial multi-chamber TPN bags over hospital-compounded TPN whenever the patient's clinical needs are met by a standard formulation, with hospital-compounded TPN reserved for patients whose nutritional requirements truly exceed what standard products can deliver. We also discussed the specific safety practices required for hospital-compounded TPN: automated compounding devices with software-enforced dose limits, mandatory pharmacist verification of every additive, structured order entry with calculation transparency, and barcode scanning of every component during compounding.

What this was important for is understanding why automation is now the default for TPN — manual compounding has too many independent failure modes, and the harm severity of any one of them is too high to accept that risk when standard commercial products are clinically equivalent for the great majority of patients. The personal piece is appreciating the historical context behind current TPN practice. The shift from manual to automated compounding was driven by specific patient deaths and institutional learning, not by abstract safety theory. Recognizing that helps me understand why the field treats TPN safety as a settled discipline rather than as an ongoing debate.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading ASPEN's TPN safety guidelines and the original case literature on the 1994 incident so I have specific examples behind the abstract safety principles; (2) shadowing a TPN compounding workflow on hospital rotation, including the automated compounding device operation, the pharmacist verification step, and the chain-of-custody documentation; and (3) understanding the criteria for hospital-compounded versus commercial multi-chamber TPN selection, so when I am eventually involved in TPN decisions I can apply the appropriate framework rather than defaulting to whatever the institution does by tradition.

Today we discussed TPN compounding errors. The 1994 Michigan TPN deaths from excess phosphate accelerated the adoption of automated compounding. ASPEN recommends commercial multi-chamber bags when clinically appropriate. Hospital-compounded TPN requires automated devices, pharmacist verification of every additive, and structured workflows. I will pay attention to TPN workflows on rotation and read ASPEN guidelines.
TPN can have errors and has caused deaths. Automation helps make it safer.

This session focused specifically on intravenous potassium administration rate as a HAM topic distinct from concentration. I learned that even appropriately diluted potassium chloride, if administered too rapidly, causes cardiac arrhythmias including ventricular fibrillation and asystole. The published maximum infusion rates are typically 10 mEq per hour through a peripheral IV and 20 mEq per hour through a central line, with continuous cardiac monitoring required for any rate above 10 mEq per hour. ISMP guidance includes standardized concentrations dispensed by pharmacy as ready-to-use products, pre-printed order sets that constrain the rate selection to validated options, smart-pump dose error reduction systems with hard limits that prevent the user from programming above-threshold rates without explicit override and documented rationale, and explicit nursing protocols for bedside potassium administration. We also discussed the broader principle that HAM safety often extends beyond the molecule itself to the administration parameters — concentration alone is necessary but not sufficient for safe potassium administration, and the rate is what determines whether a clinically appropriate concentration becomes harmful.

What this was important for is recognizing that HAM safety extends beyond dispensing into administration-rate engineering. The personal piece is realizing that smart-pump library curation — which I tend to mentally categorize as informatics work or operations work, separate from clinical pharmacy — is exactly the place where this safety boundary lives in operational form. A pharmacist who has not updated the smart-pump library to reflect the institution's potassium rate limits has effectively left a gap in the patient-safety infrastructure, regardless of how vigilant the bedside clinicians are.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing the institution's smart-pump KCl rate limits during my hospital rotation, including which units have hard limits, which have soft limits, and how often soft limits are overridden; (2) confirming that order sets enforce rate constraints by default, rather than allowing free-text rate entry that bypasses the validated options; and (3) treating any rate-override request as a stop-the-line moment requiring documented rationale, even when the request comes from a senior clinician — the override is exactly the case where the safety control is most needed.

Today's session covered IV potassium administration rate. Even appropriately diluted potassium can cause cardiac arrhythmias if given too fast. The standard rate limits are 10 mEq/hr peripheral and 20 mEq/hr central with cardiac monitoring above 10 mEq/hr. ISMP recommends standardized concentrations, pre-printed order sets, smart-pump hard limits, and nursing protocols. I will pay attention to KCl orders during rotation.
Potassium can be dangerous if given too fast. There are rate limits.

Today's class focused on concentrated lidocaine as a HAM, particularly the multiple distinct lidocaine products on the market: the topical 1% and 2% preparations used for procedures, the 4% and higher viscous oral preparations used for mucosal anesthesia, the 10% spray used in ENT procedures, and the IV cardiac lidocaine used for ventricular arrhythmias. I learned that confusion between formulations has caused harm — including a fatal pediatric case where high-concentration topical lidocaine was administered orally and another case where viscous lidocaine was used for systemic absorption-related effects rather than its labeled topical use, leading to seizures and death. ISMP guidance requires distinct labeling between formulations, separated storage so the products are physically distant from each other in pharmacy and unit stocks, prescriber-specific indication on every order, and patient-specific dispensing rather than floor stock for the higher-concentration products. We also discussed the broader principle that 'lidocaine' as a name encompasses several practically different products that pharmacists must distinguish, and that the same generic name across formulations is not a sign of clinical equivalence.

What this was important for is recognizing that medication safety often hinges on within-name distinctions that are clinical practical matters, not theoretical pharmacology. The personal piece is sharpening my habit of asking 'which lidocaine?' rather than treating the name as sufficient — and recognizing that this same discipline applies to other multi-formulation drugs (heparin, dextrose, sodium chloride, magnesium) where the same generic name covers products with very different clinical implications.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) learning the institution's complete lidocaine formulary on my rotations, including which formulations are stocked where and what indications each is used for; (2) confirming distinct labeling at storage points by spot-checking the actual locations during shadowing rather than relying on the formulary list; and (3) clarifying indication on every lidocaine order I verify, treating the absence of an indication as a verification gap rather than a stylistic preference of the prescriber.

Today's class covered concentrated lidocaine. The topical 1-2%, viscous oral 4%, ENT spray 10%, and IV cardiac forms are all called lidocaine but are clinically very different. Errors have caused harm including fatal cases. ISMP recommends distinct labeling, separated storage, indication on orders, and patient-specific dispensing for higher concentrations. I will be careful with lidocaine and will ask which formulation when verifying orders.
Lidocaine has different forms that can be confused. There have been deaths from this. I will be careful.

Today's class focused on intravenous iron preparations — iron dextran (high-molecular-weight and low-molecular-weight), ferric carboxymaltose, ferric derisomaltose, iron sucrose, and ferumoxytol — as a HAM category requiring observation for hypersensitivity reactions.
