I learned that iron dextran has historically carried the highest anaphylaxis risk and that current ISMP and ASHP guidance requires test-dose protocols for iron dextran (a small initial dose followed by an observation period before the therapeutic dose), observation periods of 30 to 60 minutes after each infusion regardless of product, and immediate epinephrine availability during every infusion encounter. The introduction of newer agents — ferric carboxymaltose, ferric derisomaltose, and iron sucrose — has reduced but not eliminated the safety concerns; serious hypersensitivity reactions still occur, and the administration setting (hospital, infusion center, or dialysis unit) determines whether emergency response is structurally available. We also discussed the labeling differences across products, the FDA black-box warning history for iron dextran, and the role of pharmacist consultation in selecting the appropriate IV iron product for a given patient given their renal function, anemia severity, and prior iron exposure history.

What this was important for is recognizing that HAM management extends to peri-administration monitoring, not just dispensing. The personal piece is understanding that I will counsel patients about IV iron in outpatient infusion settings throughout my career, and that the counseling content (signs to watch for, when to alert staff, what the observation period means) is part of the safety net rather than a courtesy. A patient who recognizes early hypersensitivity signs and alerts staff has functionally extended the institutional safety control into their own awareness; a patient who does not understand what they are watching for has functionally narrowed it.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing the institution's IV iron protocols on hospital rotation, including which products are formulary, what monitoring is required for each, and what the response algorithm is for hypersensitivity signs; (2) shadowing an outpatient infusion encounter on rotation if the opportunity arises so I see the actual administration, monitoring, and patient education sequence; and (3) building a personal counseling script for IV iron patients that covers the observation period rationale, the signs to watch for, and the action to take, so I am not improvising the conversation when I encounter it independently.

Today's class covered IV iron preparations. Iron dextran has the highest anaphylaxis risk and requires test-dose protocols. Ferric carboxymaltose, iron sucrose, and ferumoxytol have reduced but not eliminated hypersensitivity risk. All IV iron requires post-infusion observation and immediate epinephrine availability. I will be aware of IV iron safety protocols during outpatient infusion rotations.
IV iron can cause allergic reactions. Patients need to be observed after the infusion.

Today's class focused on pediatric vaccines as HAMs — both because dosing errors at the extremes of pediatric weight cause real harm, and because anaphylactic reactions, while rare, are clinically severe and require pre-positioned response protocols. I learned that ISMP and CDC vaccine-administration guidance includes patient-specific weight verification before each dose for weight-based vaccines (rare in pediatric immunization but present in some scenarios), reading the immunization record to prevent duplicate dosing or contraindicated co-administration, age-appropriate vaccine selection (DTaP versus Tdap, MMR-V versus MMR + V administered separately for the youngest age groups), and required epinephrine and observation period after administration. We also discussed the operational challenges of multi-vial vaccine clinics where multiple presentations of similar vaccines (DTaP versus Tdap, IIV versus LAIV, MMR versus MMR-V) sit physically near each other in refrigerator storage, creating LASA risk that pharmacy must mitigate through storage separation, distinct labeling, and sometimes color-coding. The class walked through the CDC's published vaccine-error categories and the most common patterns: wrong-vaccine substitutions, expired vaccine administration, and reconstitution errors.

What this was important for is recognizing that vaccines, often treated as routine in retail and immunization-clinic practice, sit in the HAM category from a system-safety perspective. The personal piece is greater discipline in vaccine workflows even when they feel low-stakes, and a clearer sense that pharmacist-administered immunization carries the same verification responsibilities as any other HAM administration, even though the cultural norms around immunization are typically faster-paced than those around inpatient HAM administration.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) practicing standardized vaccine verification on retail rotations — patient-specific record review, age and contraindication check, expiration verification, and post-administration observation — even when the immunization clinic culture treats this as overhead; (2) reviewing the CDC's vaccine error categories and the published case studies so I recognize the patterns rather than waiting to encounter them; and (3) maintaining current Basic Life Support and AED certification before practicing immunization independently, since the responsibility for managing a post-vaccine anaphylaxis event sits with the administering pharmacist.

Today's class covered pediatric vaccines as HAMs. Dosing errors at the extremes of pediatric weight and rare anaphylactic reactions are the main concerns. CDC guidance includes record review, age-appropriate selection, weight verification where applicable, and required observation after administration. Multi-vaccine clinics have LASA risk from similar vaccines stored near each other. I will be careful with vaccine administration on retail rotations.
Pediatric vaccines need careful administration. They are HAMs.

Today's class focused on code cart medication standardization across institutions. I learned that ISMP and the American Heart Association recommend a standardized national code-cart layout with consistent placement of high-risk drugs (epinephrine, atropine, sodium bicarbonate, calcium chloride, vasopressin), identical concentrations across an institution's carts (so a clinician moving between units does not encounter different concentrations of the same drug), standardized expiry-verification processes with documented sign-off, and barcode scanning of cart contents during stock checks. Variability in cart layout across hospitals and even across units within a single hospital is large and clinically meaningful — a clinician who works at multiple sites or rotates between units can be slowed in a code by an unfamiliar layout, costing seconds that translate to outcomes when ROSC depends on rapid drug administration. We also discussed published evidence that institutions adopting AHA-aligned standardized code carts experience measurable improvements in code-related medication times, and the operational challenge of maintaining standardization when individual units request modifications based on their patient population.

What this was important for is recognizing that code cart design is itself a HAM safety topic with patient-outcome implications, not a logistical or housekeeping matter. The personal piece is appreciating that the pharmacy team's design choices about cart layout shape clinician performance under stress six hours, a week, or a month later — at the moment when the design decisions are no longer visible but the consequences of them are determinative.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) requesting to inspect a code cart in detail with my preceptor on hospital rotation, including the layout rationale, the audit and restock workflow, and the documentation chain for cart maintenance; (2) comparing layouts across sites I rotate through, so I see the variation and the consequences of it firsthand; and (3) advocating for standardization within institutions when I see arbitrary variation between units' carts, since standardization is a known safety improvement that does not require new resources, only consistent decision-making.

Today we discussed code cart standardization. ISMP and AHA recommend standardized layouts with consistent drug placement and identical concentrations across an institution. Variability slows code response and costs seconds that affect outcomes. Standardized carts are associated with better code medication times. I will pay attention to code cart layout and inspect a cart with my preceptor on rotation.
Code carts should be standardized so people can find drugs fast in emergencies.

This session focused on the perioperative time-out as a HAM safety control — specifically how the time-out incorporates verification of administered medications, allergies, and antibiotic prophylaxis timing alongside the more familiar elements of site-marking and patient identification. I learned that the WHO Surgical Safety Checklist explicitly includes medication-related elements at three points in the operative encounter: sign-in (allergy verification before induction, anticipated medication needs reviewed), time-out (antibiotic prophylaxis confirmed administered within the appropriate window before incision), and sign-out (medication-related concerns reviewed before transfer of care). Adoption of the WHO checklist is associated with measurable reductions in surgical complications, including medication-related adverse events such as missed antibiotic prophylaxis, wrong-medication intraoperative administration, and perioperative anticoagulation errors. We also discussed how anesthesia HAMs — paralytics, opioids, vasopressors — get verified during the briefing rather than only at administration, and how the pharmacy team contributes to OR safety even when pharmacists are rarely physically present at the time-out.

What this was important for is connecting structured surgical safety practices to medication safety in a way I had not previously linked them. I had been thinking of the surgical time-out primarily as a wrong-site-surgery prevention tool. Today's session reframed it for me as a multifaceted safety checklist where medication elements sit alongside surgical elements, and where the pharmacy team's upstream work on order sets, antibiotic stewardship, and intraoperative medication availability is what makes the time-out's medication elements actually executable.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) observing a surgical time-out during my hospital rotation if my rotation permits, with specific attention to the medication-related elements and how they get verified in practice; (2) reviewing the WHO Surgical Safety Checklist in detail and comparing it against the institution's adapted version to see where local modifications were made; and (3) supporting the medication-related elements of the time-out through OR pharmacy work when relevant, including being available for pre-incision consultation if antibiotic timing or selection questions arise.

Today's class covered the perioperative time-out and how it includes medication verification. The WHO Surgical Safety Checklist has medication elements at sign-in, time-out, and sign-out. Adoption is associated with reduced surgical complications. Pharmacy contributes upstream through order sets, antibiotic stewardship, and intraoperative medication availability. I will think about OR safety and observe a time-out if possible.
Surgery has a time-out before starting. It includes drug verification.

Today's session focused on direct oral anticoagulant safety — apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban — including the dose-adjustment rules for renal function, the drug interactions mediated by P-glycoprotein and CYP3A4, the limited reversal options compared to warfarin, and the discontinuation-and-restart errors that cluster at procedural transitions. I learned that real-world DOAC errors cluster around incorrect dosing for renal function (especially when CrCl is borderline and the dosing adjustment threshold is not consistently applied), missed drug interactions (especially with antiepileptics, HIV antiretrovirals, and some antifungals that significantly affect DOAC plasma levels), and discontinuation-and-restart errors at procedures (failure to bridge appropriately, restart timing errors, and the not-uncommon scenario where the patient leaves the hospital with no clear instructions on when to resume their DOAC). ISMP guidance includes pharmacist-led DOAC reconciliation at every transition of care, structured patient counseling at every fill including signs of bleeding and what to do about missed doses, and explicit periprocedural protocols that specify the discontinuation interval and restart timing. The class also covered the available reversal options — idarucizumab for dabigatran, andexanet alfa for factor Xa inhibitors — and the operational reality that these agents are expensive and stocked only in larger institutions, which has implications for transfer decisions.

What this was important for is recognizing that the convenience of DOACs has shifted but not removed anticoagulation safety burden — it has just moved more of the burden to pharmacist-patient interaction in the outpatient setting and pharmacist-prescriber interaction at transitions. The personal piece is committing to substantive DOAC counseling on every retail fill rather than the brief 'any questions?' close, and to active drug-interaction screening rather than relying solely on EHR alerts that I have observed clinicians click through routinely.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) practicing teach-back DOAC counseling on every fill during my retail rotation, with specific attention to bleeding signs, missed-dose handling, and what to do before procedures; (2) building a personal renal-dose-adjustment table I can reference quickly for each DOAC, and using it actively rather than relying on the prescription as written; and (3) flagging drug interactions actively with prescribers when I see patients on a DOAC plus an interacting antiepileptic, antifungal, or antiretroviral, rather than dispensing and waiting for an adverse event.

Today's class covered DOAC safety. Real-world errors cluster around renal-function dosing, drug interactions with P-gp and CYP3A4 inhibitors, and discontinuation-restart at procedures. ISMP recommends pharmacist-led reconciliation at transitions and structured counseling at every fill. Reversal options exist (idarucizumab for dabigatran, andexanet alfa for factor Xa inhibitors) but are expensive and not universally stocked. I will counsel DOAC patients carefully on rotation.
DOACs are anticoagulants like apixaban and rivaroxaban. They have safety issues with kidney function and drug interactions.

Today's class focused on naloxone access as a HAM mitigation strategy — providing the antidote to opioid overdose alongside every opioid prescription rather than treating naloxone as a separately requested product available only on patient initiative. I learned that all 50 states now have some form of naloxone standing-order or pharmacist-prescribing authority, that bundled opioid plus naloxone counseling is now the standard of care endorsed by CDC opioid prescribing guidelines, and that pharmacist-initiated naloxone dispensing has substantially expanded community naloxone availability since the standing-order frameworks were established. The CDC opioid prescribing guidelines explicitly recommend naloxone co-prescription for patients on chronic opioid therapy, patients on doses above 50 morphine milligram equivalents per day, patients with concomitant benzodiazepine use, patients with prior overdose, and patients with substance use disorder history. We also discussed the practical workflow: identifying eligible patients during opioid dispensing, brief naloxone counseling that covers recognition of overdose signs, intranasal versus injectable administration, and the importance of involving family members or other potential responders in the conversation.

What this was important for is recognizing that naloxone access is the highest-leverage intervention pharmacists can deploy in the opioid epidemic, and that not every patient receiving an opioid leaves with the naloxone education they would benefit from. The personal piece is committing to make naloxone counseling an automatic part of opioid dispensing — not an add-on triggered by perceived risk, but a default that the patient can decline rather than a service the patient must request. Defaults shape behavior at scale, and the default of 'opioid plus naloxone counseling for everyone meeting criteria' is what shifts community-level naloxone availability.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) confirming standing-order authorization in my state and any state I work in, including the specific eligibility criteria and the documentation requirements; (2) building a naloxone counseling script with hands-on demonstration using a placebo intranasal device, so the counseling is concrete rather than abstract; and (3) tracking my own naloxone offer rate during my retail rotation as a personal quality measure, since the discipline of measurement is what converts good intentions into consistent practice.

Today's class covered naloxone access as opioid HAM mitigation. All 50 states have standing-order or pharmacist-prescribing authority. CDC recommends co-prescription for chronic opioid therapy, doses above 50 MME per day, concomitant benzodiazepines, prior overdose, and substance use disorder history. Pharmacist-initiated dispensing has expanded community access. I will offer naloxone routinely on retail rotation and build a counseling script.
Naloxone reverses opioid overdoses. Pharmacists can prescribe it under standing orders.

This session focused on concentrated dextrose D50 — 50% dextrose in water, 25 grams of dextrose per 50 mL syringe — as a HAM in hypoglycemia management. I learned that D50 administration requires central venous access or large-bore peripheral IV access because of its high osmolarity, can cause severe tissue necrosis with extravasation if administered through inadequate peripheral access, and has been associated with administration errors in pediatric patients where D10 or D25 is the appropriate concentration. ISMP guidance includes age-specific concentration availability with separate stocking of D10, D25, and D50, pediatric-specific code-cart kits that exclude D50 in favor of pediatric-appropriate concentrations, explicit administration-route requirements on every order, and clear labeling that distinguishes the available concentrations at the point of selection. We also discussed the broader principle that high-osmolarity products require infusion-route consideration as a structural safety control, not a clinical detail to be addressed at the bedside.

What this was important for is appreciating how concentration considerations extend beyond electrolytes to glucose, and how pediatric dosing differences require pharmacy-side workflow design rather than dose-by-dose vigilance from the bedside team. The personal piece is sharpening my mental model of HAM categories as broader than the canonical insulin-heparin-chemo trio — the HAM list expands to include any drug whose harm potential is shaped by concentration, route, or patient population, and the engineering controls that reduce harm look similar across that expanded list.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing the institution's D50 policy on hospital rotation, including stocking locations, code-cart inclusion, and any pediatric-specific exclusions; (2) confirming pediatric concentrations are stocked separately from adult concentrations in any unit that serves both populations; and (3) flagging D50 orders that lack explicit route specification, treating the route ambiguity as a verification gap rather than a stylistic preference.

Today we covered D50 concentrated dextrose. It needs central or large-bore peripheral IV access because of high osmolarity. Pediatric patients use D10 or D25 instead. ISMP recommends age-specific concentration availability and separate stocking. I will be aware of dextrose orders and route specification on rotation.
D50 is concentrated sugar. It can cause tissue damage if given through a small IV.

Today's class focused on investigational drug HAM management — drugs in clinical trials that lack FDA-approved labeling, full safety profiles, and standardized dispensing protocols. I learned that the FDA's investigational drug regulations under 21 CFR 312 require specific dispensing pharmacy controls including limited-access storage separate from commercial drug stocks, drug-accountability logs that track every dose by subject and visit, chain-of-custody documentation for receipt and return, and structured protocols for blinding and unblinding when applicable. Investigational drug services within hospital pharmacies are typically dedicated functions staffed by pharmacists with specific training in clinical trials regulations, GCP (Good Clinical Practice) requirements, and trial-specific documentation. The safety burden in investigational drug pharmacy is high because these drugs are often HAMs by class — oncology trial agents, cardiovascular trial agents, immunomodulators — and the investigational status itself adds uncertainty about adverse-effect profiles, drug interactions, and dose-response relationships that established commercial drugs do not carry. We also discussed the role of investigational drug services in protocol-deviation prevention, since a deviation can compromise both the patient's safety and the trial's scientific validity.

What this was important for is recognizing investigational drug services as a specialized career path with HAM safety considerations distinct from routine dispensing, and as one of the more procedurally rigorous areas of pharmacy practice. The personal piece is interest in this niche and the regulatory rigor it requires. Investigational drug pharmacy combines clinical complexity with regulatory exactness in a way that I find appealing as a career possibility.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) shadowing an investigational drug service on rotation if available, with specific attention to the accountability log workflow and the unblinding procedures; (2) reading 21 CFR 312 dispensing requirements in full so I understand the regulatory framework rather than only the operational practice; and (3) considering ASHP's investigational drug specialty tracks for residency, since this is a setting where residency training is the typical entry point and informal learning is harder to come by.

Today's class covered investigational drugs and the additional safety controls they require. 21 CFR 312 specifies dispensing requirements including limited-access storage, drug-accountability logs by subject and visit, chain-of-custody documentation, and blinding-unblinding protocols. Investigational drug services are typically staffed by pharmacists with specific training. The safety burden is high because investigational drugs are often HAMs by class with uncertain adverse-effect profiles. I will explore this niche and consider residency tracks.
Investigational drugs are in trials. They have special storage and documentation rules.

Today's session focused on patient experience as a legitimate outcome measure within IHI improvement projects, alongside the more familiar clinical metrics like A1C control, readmission rate, and mortality. I learned that IHI's Triple Aim framework — and now the expanded Quadruple Aim that adds clinician wellbeing — explicitly treats patient experience as one of three coequal outcomes, not as a soft metric subordinate to clinical results. We reviewed validated instruments including HCAHPS for inpatient settings, CG-CAHPS for ambulatory, and the IHI Patient Experience scorecard for various care settings, along with the strengths and limitations of each. Each instrument captures specific dimensions: communication with providers, responsiveness of staff, pain management, medication communication, and discharge information are core HCAHPS domains, while CG-CAHPS adds access-to-care and care-coordination dimensions. We also discussed published QI projects where using patient experience as the primary outcome produced different changes than purely clinical metrics would have driven — for example, projects that improved medication communication scores by extending counseling time, which subsequently improved adherence and clinical outcomes through the patient-experience pathway rather than directly.

What this clarified for me is that patient experience improvement is not separate from clinical pharmacy work; it is the outcome that makes our clinical work feel different to the patient and that often mediates whether the clinical change actually translates into health improvement. The personal piece was a clearer commitment to including a patient-experience measure in any QI project I run, and a sharper awareness that 'the clinical metric improved' is incomplete information about whether the project succeeded.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading the IHI Triple Aim primer and the original Berwick paper that established the framework, so I have the conceptual foundation rather than just the practical vocabulary; (2) including a patient-experience measure in my next QI proposal, with attention to which dimension is most relevant to the project's aim; and (3) reviewing HCAHPS scores at any institution where I rotate to see how the institution thinks about patient experience and whether it is integrated into operational decisions or treated as a reportable metric.

Today we discussed patient experience as an outcome measure in IHI projects. The Triple Aim framework treats patient experience as coequal to clinical outcomes and population health. HCAHPS for inpatient and CG-CAHPS for ambulatory are validated instruments that measure communication, responsiveness, pain management, and medication communication. Some QI projects use patient experience as the primary measure rather than as a supporting metric, which can drive different changes than clinical metrics alone would. I will include patient experience in my QI work and review HCAHPS scores at my rotations.
Patient experience matters in QI projects. HCAHPS measures it.

This session introduced implementation science as the academic discipline that complements IHI's pragmatic improvement model. I learned that frameworks like the Consolidated Framework for Implementation Research (CFIR), RE-AIM (Reach, Effectiveness, Adoption, Implementation, Maintenance), and PARiHS (Promoting Action on Research Implementation in Health Services) provide structured ways to assess why interventions succeed in some contexts and fail in others. CFIR organizes implementation determinants into five domains: intervention characteristics, outer setting, inner setting, characteristics of individuals, and the implementation process itself. RE-AIM emphasizes the multi-dimensional nature of an intervention's real-world impact, distinguishing between an intervention's efficacy in ideal conditions and its implementability in routine practice. The class walked through several examples where IHI improvement projects with strong PDSA results in their pilot setting failed to spread because the implementation context was not analyzed in advance — interventions that depended on a particular electronic health record configuration, on a specific staffing model, or on leadership support that was unique to the pilot site.

What this clarified for me is why some IHI projects with strong pilot results don't spread — the framework didn't account for context. The personal piece is recognizing that being a successful pharmacist-improver requires fluency in both pragmatic IHI tools and the implementation science vocabulary that diagnoses why work travels or doesn't. The two disciplines are complementary: IHI tells you how to test changes; implementation science tells you why the same change might work here and not there.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading one CFIR overview paper and Damschroder's original 2009 framework article so I have the foundational reference rather than only secondary descriptions; (2) using a context analysis (barriers, facilitators, key stakeholders) before any change-spread effort I am part of, so the implementation determinants are explicit rather than implicit; and (3) framing my QI work outputs in implementation-science terms when communicating to leadership, since that vocabulary is increasingly what funders, accreditors, and senior leaders use.

Today we covered implementation science. CFIR, RE-AIM, and PARiHS are frameworks that explain why interventions spread or fail. CFIR organizes determinants into five domains including intervention characteristics, outer setting, inner setting, individuals, and process. Implementation science complements IHI's pragmatic model by analyzing context. I'll learn the frameworks and use context analysis on my projects.
Implementation science studies why things spread or fail. There are frameworks for it.

Today's class applied IHI improvement methodology to sepsis bundle compliance — specifically the SEP-1 measure that CMS has tied to hospital reimbursement under the Hospital Inpatient Quality Reporting program. I learned that the SEP-1 bundle includes lactate measurement within three hours of presentation, blood cultures drawn before antibiotic administration, broad-spectrum antibiotics within one hour of severe sepsis or septic shock recognition, IV fluids (30 mL/kg) for hypotension or lactate elevation, and vasopressors for refractory hypotension after fluid resuscitation. The class walked through published QI literature showing that pharmacist-led order sets, antibiotic timing alerts, and pharmacist participation in sepsis response teams have been the highest-leverage interventions across institutions, with measurable improvements in SEP-1 compliance and corresponding reductions in mortality. We also discussed the operational reality that SEP-1 compliance requires coordination across the emergency department, ICU, and inpatient units, and that the pharmacist's role differs by setting — antibiotic dispensing speed in the ED, dose appropriateness in the ICU, and antimicrobial stewardship oversight across the entire continuum.

This was important because sepsis bundle compliance is a near-universal hospital QI priority and one where pharmacist contribution is direct and measurable. The personal piece is recognizing this as a high-impact entry point for early-career QI work — sepsis is high-volume, the bundle elements are well-defined, the data infrastructure already exists in most institutions, and pharmacist-led interventions have published evidence behind them.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing the SEP-1 bundle elements in detail and the most recent CMS specification so I understand what is actually being measured; (2) shadowing the sepsis stewardship team on inpatient rotation if the institution has one, with specific attention to how the pharmacist's role is structured and how performance is tracked; and (3) proposing a focused PDSA on antibiotic timing if my rotation permits — for example, comparing ED time-to-antibiotic for sepsis cases identified through screening versus through clinical judgment.

Today's class covered sepsis bundle compliance as an IHI improvement target. SEP-1 is tied to CMS reimbursement and includes lactate measurement, blood cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum antibiotics within one hour, IV fluids, and vasopressors for refractory hypotension. Pharmacist-led order sets and antibiotic timing alerts are high-leverage interventions. I will learn about sepsis QI and shadow the stewardship team on rotation.
Sepsis has a bundle of interventions. Compliance is important and CMS measures it.

Today's session focused on venous thromboembolism (VTE) prophylaxis as an IHI improvement target. I learned that hospital-acquired VTE — including deep venous thrombosis and pulmonary embolism — is among the most common preventable causes of in-hospital death, and that bundle-compliant prophylaxis (risk-stratified pharmacologic prophylaxis with low-molecular-weight heparin or unfractionated heparin, plus mechanical prophylaxis with sequential compression devices when appropriate) substantially reduces VTE incidence. We reviewed published QI projects that drove VTE prophylaxis rates from baseline 50-60% up to 90%+ using a combination of order-set redesign that defaulted to risk-stratified prophylaxis, pharmacist-led missed-dose follow-up, EHR best-practice alerts at admission and at transfer, and explicit documentation requirements when prophylaxis is not provided. The class also covered the role of risk-stratification tools (Caprini score for surgical patients, Padua score for medical patients) in moving the conversation from 'should we provide prophylaxis' to 'which prophylaxis is appropriate for this patient's risk profile.'

What this was important for is understanding that VTE prophylaxis is one of the highest-volume opportunities for pharmacist contribution to inpatient quality. The personal piece is appreciating that small process improvements — a better order set default, an earlier alert at admission, a more reliable missed-dose follow-up — can cumulatively prevent multiple deaths per institution per year. This is exactly the kind of work that doesn't generate dramatic improvement stories but does generate dramatic harm reduction.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing the institution's VTE risk-stratification protocol on hospital rotation, including which scoring tool is used and how the prophylaxis decision flows from the score; (2) tracking missed-dose rates if my rotation permits, since missed doses are the largest single contributor to prophylaxis failure; and (3) flagging VTE prophylaxis gaps as part of routine verification, treating the absence of prophylaxis on a high-risk patient as a verification finding rather than a clinical decision I should defer to the prescribing service.

Today we covered VTE prophylaxis as a QI target. Hospital-acquired VTE is a major preventable cause of in-hospital death. Order set redesign, pharmacist follow-up on missed doses, and EHR alerts can drive prophylaxis rates from 50-60% to 90%+. Risk-stratification tools like Caprini and Padua guide which prophylaxis is appropriate. I'll pay attention to VTE prophylaxis on rotation and track missed doses where possible.
VTE prophylaxis prevents clots in hospitalized patients. It's important.

Today's class examined hand hygiene compliance as one of the most-studied and most-stubborn QI targets in healthcare. I learned that direct observation by trained observers is the gold-standard measurement method but is expensive and subject to observation bias (the Hawthorne effect — clinicians wash their hands more when being watched). Alternative approaches include secret shopper observation, electronic hand-hygiene monitoring systems that track entries and exits with badge taps and dispenser activation, and consumption-based measurement (tracking soap and sanitizer volume consumed per patient-day). Sustained compliance above 80% requires multilayered interventions — adequate dispenser placement and supply, workflow design that doesn't penalize hand hygiene, leadership modeling of consistent behavior, peer-to-peer accountability, and explicit reward or recognition systems — none of which alone is sufficient. The class also reviewed the published evidence that hand hygiene compliance improvements correlate with reductions in healthcare-associated infections including MRSA, VRE, and C. difficile.

What this clarified for me is that the simplest-seeming QI target is often the hardest, because behavior change at scale is fundamentally difficult. The personal piece was understanding that hand hygiene is the first project most QI teams fail at, and that a more humble starting expectation is appropriate. Improvement projects that promise rapid hand-hygiene compliance gains are typically either measuring something else or are about to disappoint.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) observing my own hand hygiene compliance on rotation, since the discipline of self-monitoring is what most consistent compliers have in common; (2) reviewing the institution's hand-hygiene measurement approach and noting whether it relies on observation or electronic systems; and (3) modeling hand hygiene visibly in any team I'm part of — the social signal of consistent compliance from peers is the most reliable single intervention.

Today's class covered hand hygiene compliance. Direct observation is the gold standard but is expensive and subject to Hawthorne effect. Multilayered interventions are needed because no single change drives sustained improvement. Behavior change at scale is hard. I'll model hand hygiene visibly at my rotations.
Hand hygiene is hard to improve. It's still important for patient safety.

Today's class focused on inpatient falls as an IHI improvement target with strong pharmacy involvement — particularly through medication review for fall-risk-increasing drugs, often abbreviated as FRIDs. I learned that the canonical FRID classes include benzodiazepines, non-benzodiazepine sedative hypnotics, anticholinergics (especially first-generation antihistamines and tricyclic antidepressants), antihypertensives that produce orthostatic hypotension, antipsychotics, and opioids. Pharmacist-led FRID review at hospital admission and discharge has been associated with reduced fall rates in multiple QI publications, with the strongest evidence in older adults and in patients with prior fall history. The intervention is conceptually simple but operationally difficult because deprescribing requires prescriber buy-in, patient counseling about why a long-standing medication is being stopped or changed, and clear handoff to outpatient providers about why the change was made and how to monitor for return of the original symptoms. We also discussed the AGS Beers Criteria as the most widely used reference for inappropriate prescribing in older adults, including specific FRID flags and the rationale behind each.

What this was important for is recognizing fall reduction as an interdisciplinary improvement target where pharmacy contributes specifically through medication review, not as a generic 'safety' contributor. The personal piece is realizing that deprescribing is a clinical pharmacy skill that grows over a career and is worth deliberate development — it requires both the pharmacology knowledge to know what to deprescribe and the communication skills to make the deprescribing conversation productive rather than adversarial.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) learning the institution's FRID list and review process on hospital rotation, including how the review is triggered and how the recommendations are communicated to prescribers; (2) practicing deprescribing conversations with my preceptor in low-stakes situations before I am responsible for them independently; and (3) reading the AGS Beers Criteria as a core reference, with specific attention to the FRID classes and the strength-of-evidence ratings.

Today's session covered fall reduction QI. Pharmacist FRID review at admission and discharge reduces falls. Canonical FRID classes include benzodiazepines, sedative hypnotics, anticholinergics, antihypertensives, antipsychotics, and opioids. Deprescribing requires prescriber buy-in and patient counseling. I'll learn the institution's FRID list and read the AGS Beers Criteria.
Falls happen in hospitals. Some drugs increase the risk. Pharmacists review them.

Today's session focused on opioid stewardship as a pain-management QI target structurally analogous to antimicrobial stewardship. I learned that ASHP and CDC frameworks include multimodal pain management protocols that prioritize non-opioid analgesics and adjuvants, MME-based prescribing limits with structured override pathways, automatic naloxone co-prescription for high-risk patients, structured pharmacist consultation for opioid-prescribing reviews especially at care transitions, and explicit policies for chronic opioid initiation that include treatment agreements and PDMP review. Opioid stewardship interventions have produced measurable reductions in chronic opioid initiation rates, post-surgical opioid duration, and ED opioid prescribing in published projects. We also discussed the parallels with antimicrobial stewardship — both disciplines balance individual patient need against population-level harm, both require structured oversight to constrain inappropriate prescribing without blocking appropriate use, and both depend on pharmacist clinical leadership.

What this was important for is recognizing opioid stewardship as a discipline parallel to antimicrobial stewardship — same logic, similar methods, different drug class, but with the same career-development implications for pharmacists who pursue it. The personal piece is interest in being the kind of pharmacist who pursues stewardship roles, since these positions combine clinical depth with system-level impact in a way that's professionally rewarding and high-impact.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing CDC opioid prescribing guidelines and the most recent ASHP statement on pain management stewardship; (2) shadowing the institution's pain management team or stewardship team during rotation if available; and (3) considering opioid stewardship as a residency focus or early-career role, since the field is growing rapidly and entry-level positions are increasingly available.

Today's class covered opioid stewardship as a pain-management QI target. CDC and ASHP frameworks include multimodal pain management, MME prescribing limits, naloxone co-prescription, pharmacist consultation, and structured policies for chronic opioid initiation. Stewardship interventions have reduced chronic opioid initiation, post-surgical duration, and ED prescribing. I'll learn opioid stewardship and consider it as a residency focus.
Opioid stewardship reduces inappropriate opioid use. It's like antimicrobial stewardship.

Today's class focused on smoking cessation as a community pharmacy QI target with clear pharmacist scope and strong evidence base. I learned that pharmacist-led brief cessation counseling using the 5 A's framework (Ask about tobacco use, Advise about quitting, Assess readiness to change, Assist with cessation including pharmacotherapy and counseling, and Arrange follow-up), combined with NRT recommendations and prescription support for varenicline or bupropion when appropriate, has measurable quit-rate impact in published trials. Several states authorize pharmacist prescribing of cessation aids under collaborative practice agreements or standing-order authority, which expands access by removing the prescription barrier for patients ready to quit. We also discussed the role of e-cigarettes in cessation conversations — the public health debate about whether e-cigarettes are net-beneficial as harm-reduction or net-harmful as continued nicotine addiction, and how pharmacists should approach this in counseling.

What this was important for is recognizing that community pharmacists are uniquely positioned for cessation counseling — frequent patient contact, accessibility without appointment, established trust over multiple encounters, and ability to integrate cessation into routine prescription pickups. The personal piece is committing to do brief cessation counseling routinely rather than treating it as someone else's responsibility, since the evidence is clear that brief pharmacist counseling produces real quit attempts.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) memorizing the 5 A's framework and practicing it in low-stakes encounters during retail rotation; (2) verifying my state's pharmacist cessation prescribing authority — whether prescribing is under standing order, collaborative practice agreement, or full prescriptive authority; and (3) tracking my own cessation conversations during retail rotation as a personal quality measure, since the discipline of measurement is what converts intention into consistent behavior.

Today we covered smoking cessation as a pharmacy QI target. The 5 A's framework guides counseling: Ask, Advise, Assess, Assist, Arrange. Some states authorize pharmacist prescribing of cessation aids under standing orders or collaborative practice agreements. Brief pharmacist counseling has measurable quit-rate impact. I'll do cessation counseling routinely on retail rotation.
Pharmacists can help patients quit smoking. There's a 5 A's framework.

Today's class examined how CMS value-based payment programs create financial incentives for the QI work pharmacists do, and how fluency in payment-program vocabulary is increasingly part of pharmacy career advancement. I learned the major programs: the Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program (HRRP) that penalizes hospitals for excess readmissions in defined conditions, the Hospital-Acquired Conditions Reduction Program (HACRP) that penalizes high HAC rates, the Merit-based Incentive Payment System (MIPS) for clinician reimbursement, and the Pharmacy Quality Alliance (PQA) measures used in Medicare Part D Star Ratings that determine pharmacy plan reimbursement. The class walked through how VBP-aligned interventions translate clinical pharmacy value into ROI that gets services funded — a transitions-of-care pharmacist who reduces readmissions saves the institution substantially more than the position costs, and articulating that ROI is what builds a credible business case in management meetings. The skills required to make this case are different from purely clinical skills and are not always taught explicitly in pharmacy curricula.

What this was important for is recognizing that pharmacy career trajectory increasingly requires fluency in payment models, not just clinical knowledge. The personal piece is committing to learn this vocabulary even though it's outside the typical pharmacy curriculum, since pharmacists who can speak both clinically and financially are the ones who get clinical pharmacy positions funded and who advance into senior roles.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading CMS VBP program summaries — HRRP, HACRP, MIPS, and Star Ratings — so I understand the financial incentives shaping institutional priorities; (2) asking institutional preceptors how their roles connect to VBP and which programs influence their day-to-day work; and (3) building a vocabulary of payment-program acronyms and core concepts I can use credibly in management conversations.

Today we covered value-based payment programs and how they fund pharmacy services. HRRP, HACRP, MIPS, and Star Ratings each create incentives for QI work. Pharmacists who can articulate ROI in payment-program terms get clinical positions funded. I'll learn the payment vocabulary and ask preceptors how their roles connect to VBP.
Payment programs affect pharmacy. CMS measures things.

Today's session focused on patient safety dashboards as a structural QI tool that converts data into management action. I learned that effective dashboards include leading indicators (process measures and near-miss reports that signal upcoming risk), lagging indicators (sentinel events, harm rates that reflect realized harm), trended over time with control limits that distinguish signal from noise, segmented by unit so the right team owns the right metric, and tied to specific accountable owners so accountability is operational rather than diffuse. Dashboards that hide bad data, omit balancing measures, or show too many metrics fail at their purpose because they either obscure the safety reality or overwhelm the user. We discussed the design choices that distinguish effective dashboards from ineffective ones: appropriate level of detail for the audience (executives need different dashboards than unit managers), explicit thresholds that trigger response, and integration into the management rhythm rather than passive reporting.

What this was important for is recognizing that the dashboard's design is itself the QI message — what the dashboard chooses to display, how it displays it, and what it omits all shape what gets attention. Dashboards shape behavior at scale because they shape attention at scale. The personal piece is appreciating dashboard design as a clinical-leadership skill I'll need, and one that combines clinical judgment with data literacy and communication design.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing the institution's safety dashboard if accessible during rotations, with specific attention to the design choices and their implications; (2) studying its design choices critically rather than only reading the data, since the design tells you what the institution thinks matters; and (3) drafting an example dashboard for one workflow as a learning exercise, including the leading and lagging indicators I would track and the thresholds I would set.

Today's class covered patient safety dashboards. Good dashboards include leading and lagging indicators, are trended with control limits, segmented by unit, and tied to accountable owners. Bad dashboards hide data, omit balancing measures, or show too much. The design itself is the QI message. I'll think about dashboard design and review the institution's dashboard on rotation.
Dashboards show safety data. They have indicators.

Today's class explored Health Information Exchange (HIE) as a QI infrastructure topic that increasingly limits what improvement projects are feasible. I learned that interoperable patient data — flowing across hospitals, pharmacies, and outpatient practices — enables QI projects that purely intra-institutional data cannot support, especially around transitions of care, medication reconciliation across settings, and population health management for chronic disease. The Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology (ONC) interoperability standards, including FHIR APIs (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) and the United States Core Data for Interoperability (USCDI), are the technical foundation for cross-system data exchange. We discussed how the 21st Century Cures Act information-blocking provisions create a regulatory floor for data sharing that institutions and EHR vendors must meet, and how that regulatory environment is reshaping what cross-organizational QI projects are technically feasible.

What this was important for is recognizing that QI projects are increasingly limited by data infrastructure rather than by intervention design — the bottleneck for many cross-setting improvements is the data flow, not the clinical idea. The personal piece is interest in pharmacy informatics roles where this infrastructure is built and maintained, and recognizing that pharmacy informatics is one of the fastest-growing pharmacy specialties.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) learning what HIE my future state participates in and what data flows through it; (2) understanding the FHIR API basics at a conceptual level so I can engage informatics conversations credibly; and (3) considering pharmacy informatics tracks for residency, since the field is short on pharmacists with both clinical and technical depth.

Today we covered HIE and QI. Interoperability through FHIR APIs and USCDI standards enables cross-setting QI projects. The 21st Century Cures Act information-blocking provisions create a regulatory floor for data sharing. I'll learn about HIE in my future state and consider pharmacy informatics for residency.
HIE shares data between organizations. It helps QI projects.

Today's class focused on CMS quality reporting frameworks and especially the Pharmacy Quality Alliance (PQA) measures used in Medicare Part D Star Ratings. I learned that PQA measures including proportion of days covered (PDC) for chronic medications like statins, ARBs, and oral diabetes medications, statin use in persons with diabetes (SUPD), high-risk medication use in older adults (HRM), and several others directly determine pharmacy plan ratings, which in turn determine plan enrollment, member retention, and pharmacy reimbursement under Star-Ratings-tied performance contracts. The PQA measures are essentially a QI scorecard for community pharmacy, even though many community pharmacists do not consciously frame their work in PQA terms. We also discussed the broader CMS quality reporting infrastructure including Hospital Compare, the SEP-1 measure for sepsis, MIPS for outpatient clinicians, and the relationships between these programs.

What this was important for is recognizing that the metrics community pharmacists operate against are largely PQA measures, whether the pharmacist names them or not. Performance review, bonus structures, and management priorities at most chain pharmacies are PQA-driven, and pharmacists who understand the measures can engage management conversations more credibly than those who don't. The personal piece is making PQA fluency part of my early-career skill set rather than learning it after I am already operating against the measures.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) memorizing the major PQA measures relevant to community pharmacy and the calculation methodology for each; (2) tracking my chain's Star Ratings and understanding how my individual work contributes; and (3) framing my MTM and counseling work in PQA terms so I am operating with intention rather than accidentally.

Today's session covered CMS reporting and PQA measures that drive Star Ratings. PDC for chronic medications, SUPD, and HRM are core measures. Star Ratings determine plan reimbursement. Community pharmacy operates against these measures whether or not pharmacists name them. I'll learn PQA and track my chain's ratings.
CMS has quality measures. Pharmacies are rated on them.

Today's class examined pediatric asthma as a QI target with multi-setting pharmacy involvement spanning outpatient, retail, and emergency-department settings. I learned that effective QI projects in pediatric asthma combine controller adherence interventions (refill-based monitoring, medication therapy management visits, automated reminders), inhaler technique training (which is poor in published audits — most patients use their inhaler incorrectly even after multiple counseling encounters), pharmacist-developed asthma action plans that translate the prescriber's plan into patient-actionable language, and post-discharge follow-up after ED visits. The IHI framework adapted to pediatric asthma typically uses controller-to-rescuer ratio as a process measure (since over-reliance on rescue medication signals poor control), ED visit rate or hospitalization rate as outcome measures, and patient-reported asthma control instruments like the Asthma Control Test (ACT) for symptom-based outcomes. We reviewed published QI projects that improved pediatric asthma outcomes through pharmacist-led interventions and the role of GINA (Global Initiative for Asthma) guidelines as the clinical foundation.

What this was important for is recognizing pediatric asthma as a chronic-disease QI exemplar that I'll encounter in nearly any community or ambulatory setting. The personal piece is interest in pediatric pharmacy work, including the inhaler-technique counseling that is deceptively important and is one of the cleanest opportunities for measurable patient-impact work in retail pharmacy.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading the GINA pediatric asthma guidelines and the ACT instrument; (2) practicing inhaler-technique counseling with a placebo device on rotation, including teach-back; and (3) considering a pediatric-focused early career role.

Today we covered pediatric asthma QI. Controller adherence, inhaler technique, asthma action plans, and post-ED follow-up are key intervention areas. The controller-to-rescuer ratio is a process measure. ED visits and hospitalizations are outcomes. ACT is a patient-reported instrument. I'll work on inhaler-technique counseling and read GINA guidelines.
Asthma in kids needs management. Pharmacists help with inhalers.

Today's class focused on heart failure guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) optimization as one of the most evidence-rich pharmacy QI areas in cardiovascular medicine. I learned that the four pillars of GDMT for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) are an angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor (ARNI), or ACE inhibitor or ARB if ARNI is not tolerated; an evidence-based beta-blocker (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, or bisoprolol); a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (spironolactone or eplerenone); and an SGLT2 inhibitor. These four classes are widely under-titrated in real-world practice — published audits show that fewer than 20% of HFrEF patients are at target doses across all four classes, even though each class has independent mortality benefit and the combination is far more effective than partial regimens. Pharmacist-led titration clinics have published evidence of dramatic improvements in target-dose attainment, with corresponding reductions in heart-failure hospitalizations and mortality. We discussed the operational structure of these clinics — typically a collaborative practice agreement with cardiology that gives the pharmacist titration authority within defined parameters — and the workflow elements (laboratory monitoring, blood pressure assessment, symptom assessment) that distinguish pharmacist-managed titration from prescriber-managed titration.

What this was important for is recognizing heart failure GDMT as a near-perfect application of pharmacist clinical work — high-evidence, gap-rich, amenable to protocol-driven titration, and producing measurable mortality benefit. The personal piece is interest in heart failure clinical work as a possible career direction, since the practice combines clinical depth with measurable patient-level impact in a way that few other ambulatory practices match.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading the AHA/ACC HF guidelines and the most recent ESC heart failure guidelines for comparison; (2) shadowing a heart failure clinic if rotation permits; and (3) considering ambulatory heart failure roles for early career.

Today's class covered HF GDMT optimization. The four pillars are ARNI/ACEI/ARB, beta-blocker, MRA, and SGLT2 inhibitor. Most patients are under-titrated despite each class having mortality benefit. Pharmacist-led titration clinics improve target-dose attainment and reduce hospitalizations. I'll read AHA/ACC guidelines and consider ambulatory HF roles.
Heart failure has guidelines for medications. Pharmacists help with titration.

Today's session focused on statin underutilization in eligible cardiovascular and diabetic patients as a community pharmacy QI target with direct connection to PQA Star Ratings. I learned that the PQA Statin Use in Persons with Diabetes (SUPD) measure tracks the proportion of patients aged 40-75 with diabetes who are on a statin, and the Statin Therapy for Patients with Cardiovascular Disease (SPC) measure tracks statin use in patients with established ASCVD. Both measures are part of Medicare Part D Star Ratings, and gaps in attainment have direct financial implications for pharmacy plans and indirect implications for individual pharmacy contracts. Pharmacist outreach to identify and recommend statin initiation in eligible-but-untreated patients has measurable impact on attainment of these measures, and the intervention is well within community pharmacy scope: identify eligibility from refill records, recommend appropriate statin to the prescriber, and counsel the patient on initiation.

What this was important for is recognizing statin underuse as a population-health gap I can address from a community pharmacy desk, with direct connection to a measurable quality metric. The personal piece is committing to identify and act on these gaps rather than waiting for prescribers, since pharmacist initiative is exactly what closes the gap that prescriber-initiated workflow has failed to close.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) learning the PQA SUPD and SPC criteria in detail, including the inclusion and exclusion rules; (2) tracking statin use among my rotation's diabetic patients during retail rotation as a learning exercise; and (3) making one statin recommendation per shift to a prescriber when criteria are met, treating the recommendation as an expected clinical contribution rather than overstepping.

Today's class covered statin underuse. PQA SUPD measures statin use in diabetes and SPC measures statin use in ASCVD. Both are Star Ratings measures with financial implications. Pharmacist outreach to recommend statin initiation closes the gap. I'll work on identifying eligible-untreated patients on rotation.
Statins prevent cardiovascular disease. Many patients should be on them but aren't.

Today's final IMI session focused on health equity as an explicit QI target — the discipline of stratifying every project's outcome data by race, ethnicity, language, socioeconomic status, and other equity-relevant variables to expose disparities that aggregate data hides. I learned that IHI's Pursuing Equity initiative requires QI teams to set equity-specific aim statements (not just overall improvement aims) and balancing measures that explicitly track whether the intervention is closing or widening disparities. Without stratification, an improvement project can produce overall mean improvement that masks widening inequity — the average goes up because well-served patients improve, while poorly-served patients see no change or worsen. We reviewed several published cases where adherence rates rose for English-speaking patients while remaining flat for Spanish-speaking patients, and where the disparity was invisible until the team disaggregated the data. The class emphasized that equity in QI is not a separate exercise from clinical improvement; it is a frame applied to clinical improvement to ensure that the work is benefiting the populations who most need it.

What this was important for is recognizing that QI without equity stratification can produce average improvement that hides inequitable outcomes. The personal piece is committing to stratify routinely, not as an afterthought added at the end of a project but as a design choice from the start. This is one of the cleanest applications of structural-justice thinking to clinical practice — the structural choice (to stratify) determines whether the equity work actually happens.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading the IHI Pursuing Equity primer and at least one case study; (2) including equity stratification in any QI proposal I draft; and (3) raising equity stratification as a question if it's missing in projects I review, since the question itself is the lowest-cost way to introduce the discipline.

Today's class covered health equity as a QI target. IHI Pursuing Equity stratifies outcomes by race, language, and socioeconomic status to expose disparities that aggregate data hides. Without stratification, average improvement can mask widening inequity. I'll include equity stratification in any QI projects I work on.
Equity matters in QI. I'll think about it in projects.

Today's class focused on patient handoffs as a primary patient safety vulnerability across every clinical setting. I learned that the typical inpatient passes through 8 to 15 handoffs per admission — between shifts, between care teams (medical, surgical, ICU, ED), between physical units (medical floor to ICU and back), and at discharge — and that information loss at handoffs is implicated in a substantial portion of preventable adverse events. Standardized handoff tools have published evidence of reducing adverse events when implemented with fidelity: I-PASS (Illness severity, Patient summary, Action list, Situation awareness and contingency plans, Synthesis by receiver) for inpatient resident-to-resident handoffs, SBAR (Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation) for situational handoffs, and structured nursing change-of-shift sheets for nursing transitions. The Joint Commission has identified handoffs as a sentinel-event-related communication failure category in over 80% of root cause analyses, which makes handoff design one of the highest-leverage safety interventions available. We discussed how pharmacist-to-pharmacist handoffs at shift change are typically less structured than physician handoffs, and how that gap is one of the under-recognized contributors to medication errors at care transitions.

What this clarified for me is that handoff design is a primary patient safety control, not a soft skill or a courtesy. The personal piece is recognizing that the handoff I give as a junior pharmacist sets the standard others will see — if I model structured handoffs from the start of my career, I am contributing to safety culture even before I have any formal authority to shape it.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) using a structured handoff template at every shift transition during my rotations, even when the receiving pharmacist would accept a less structured handoff; (2) practicing I-PASS with my preceptor in low-stakes contexts so the discipline becomes natural before I am responsible for it independently; and (3) treating handoff feedback as professional development rather than personal critique, since handoff competence is a skill that grows with deliberate practice.

Today's class covered patient handoffs as a safety vulnerability. The typical inpatient has 8-15 handoffs per admission and information loss at handoffs contributes to a substantial portion of preventable adverse events. I-PASS, SBAR, and structured nursing change-of-shift sheets reduce errors when used with fidelity. The Joint Commission identifies handoff communication failures in over 80% of sentinel-event RCAs. I'll use structured handoffs at every transition during rotations.
Handoffs can lose information between shifts and teams. This causes errors. I'll use structured tools.

This session focused on bedside multidisciplinary rounds as a structural patient safety practice. I learned that including the patient and family in rounds, when they wish to be included, increases medication-list accuracy, surfaces unspoken concerns and questions, and reduces post-discharge medication errors. Pharmacist participation in rounds is among the highest-yield clinical pharmacy activities in inpatient practice and is documented in multiple studies as reducing prescribing errors, ADEs, and length of stay. The mechanism is not mysterious — pharmacists in rounds catch dosing errors, suggest therapeutic alternatives, identify drug interactions, and clarify patient questions in real time, with the entire team present so the recommendations and decisions are heard by everyone who will execute them. We also discussed why pharmacist rounds participation is uneven across institutions: staffing limitations, traditional role boundaries, and prescriber receptivity all play roles. The institutions where pharmacists round consistently are typically those where pharmacy leadership invested in the practice deliberately rather than waiting for prescribers to invite participation.

What this was important for is recognizing rounds participation as a safety control with named outcomes, not as a credentialing exercise or a status symbol. The personal piece is committing to substantive rounds participation rather than passive observation when I have the opportunity, since the value of the practice depends entirely on whether the pharmacist is contributing or just present.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) attending rounds at every inpatient rotation where the team permits, including specifically asking to round on services where pharmacists don't traditionally round; (2) preparing one specific clinical recommendation per patient before rounds, so my contribution is grounded in chart review rather than improvised; and (3) practicing concise, evidence-based contributions when called on — the typical effective rounds contribution is 30 seconds with a clear recommendation, not a lecture.

Today's class covered bedside rounds. Patient and family participation increases medication-list accuracy and reduces post-discharge errors. Pharmacist participation reduces prescribing errors, ADEs, and length of stay. I'll attend rounds at every inpatient rotation and prepare clinical recommendations beforehand.
Rounds at the bedside help with safety and patient communication.

Today's class focused on emergency department safety challenges — a uniquely high-acuity, high-interruption environment with weight-based dosing required for every pediatric patient regardless of verified weight, verbal orders during resuscitation that bypass standard prescribing safeguards, and diagnostic uncertainty that requires medication decisions before the diagnostic picture is complete. I learned that ED-specific medication safety controls include weight-in-kilograms-only practice (banning pound-based weights from pediatric workflow because conversion errors have caused harm), smart pump templates pre-configured for ED bolus drugs (so the rate and concentration are constrained at the pump level), pharmacist-in-ED programs that place a clinical pharmacist physically in the department during peak hours, and structured signout to admitting teams when patients are admitted from the ED. We also discussed the published evidence on ED clinical pharmacy programs — measurable reductions in medication errors, faster antibiotic-time-to-administration in sepsis, and improved adherence to ED-specific protocols when pharmacists are embedded.

What this was important for is recognizing ED pharmacy as a distinct discipline requiring different reflexes than inpatient or outpatient pharmacy. The pace, the verbal-order culture, the need to make medication decisions with incomplete information — these are not deficits to be corrected by importing inpatient norms but features of the practice that ED pharmacists learn to navigate. The personal piece is interest in ED pharmacy as a possible early-career role, since the work is fast-paced, high-impact, and increasingly recognized as a distinct specialty.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) requesting an ED pharmacy rotation if my program permits; (2) reading the ASHP statement on emergency medicine pharmacist services and the published evidence on ED pharmacy outcomes; and (3) practicing rapid-response medication math in low-stakes settings so the calculations are automatic rather than effortful when speed matters.

Today's class covered ED safety challenges. High acuity, frequent interruptions, weight-based dosing, verbal orders, and diagnostic uncertainty create unique risks. ED-specific controls include weight-in-kg-only practice, pre-configured smart pump templates, pharmacist-in-ED programs, and structured admission signout. I'll request an ED rotation and read the ASHP emergency medicine statement.
EDs are busy and chaotic. Errors happen. ED pharmacists help.

This session focused on intensive care safety — patients on multiple high-alert medications simultaneously, high alarm density that contributes to alarm fatigue, frequent interruptions during medication preparation and administration, and exceptionally low margins for error given the underlying severity of illness. I learned that ICU patients receive on average 1.7 medications per hour, that the medication mix typically includes multiple infusions plus scheduled and PRN doses, and that ICU pharmacist consultation has measurable mortality benefit in randomized data. The canonical ICU safety controls include standardized concentration drips dispensed by central pharmacy rather than mixed at the bedside, smart pump libraries with ICU-specific limits and concentrations, daily multidisciplinary rounds with pharmacist participation, protocol-driven sedation and analgesia (so the titration follows institutional protocol rather than clinician preference), and structured medication reconciliation at every transfer in or out of the unit. We also discussed the differences between medical, surgical, neurological, and cardiac ICUs, and why some ICU types have particularly dense pharmacy involvement.

What this was important for is appreciating ICU pharmacy as a high-leverage clinical discipline with substantial published evidence behind pharmacist involvement. The personal piece is interest in critical care residency tracks, since the work combines pharmacology depth, real-time clinical decision-making, and direct patient impact in a way that is professionally rewarding.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) requesting a critical care rotation during my advanced experiential year; (2) reading the ASHP critical care pharmacy practice statement and the major published evidence on ICU pharmacist outcomes; and (3) studying ICU-specific drip protocols (vasopressors, sedation, paralytics, anticoagulation) so the foundational pharmacology is solid before the rotation begins.

Today's class covered ICU safety. Multiple HAMs, alarm density, interruptions, and acuity create high risk. ICU pharmacist consultation has measurable mortality benefit. Standardized concentrations, smart pump libraries, multidisciplinary rounds, and protocol-driven sedation are core controls. I'll request a critical care rotation and study ICU drip protocols.
ICUs are high-risk environments. Pharmacists help with medication safety there.

Today's class focused on perioperative medication safety — a HAM-dense environment with anesthesia agents, paralytics, vasopressors, antibiotics, and antiemetics all administered in close succession during surgery. I learned that the OR has historically been a 'closed' environment where pharmacist presence was limited to the central pharmacy supplying the OR rather than embedded in the OR itself. ASHP and AHRQ have advocated for expanded OR pharmacy roles, and several institutions now embed pharmacists in surgical services with measurable outcomes around antibiotic prophylaxis timing (which directly affects surgical site infection rates), drug shortage management for OR-specific products, controlled substance reconciliation (since the OR is a high-volume controlled substance environment with structurally complex documentation requirements), and intraoperative medication consultation. The class also walked through the OR-specific medication safety controls — anesthesia drug labeling standards, syringe color-coding by drug class, the perioperative time-out as a medication-verification moment, and structured handoff to PACU at case end.

What this was important for is recognizing OR pharmacy as a developing discipline with safety-driven justification rather than as a role retrofitted into a traditionally non-pharmacy space. The personal piece is curiosity about working in this environment, where the pharmacist's role is being actively defined and where there is professional space to shape the role rather than inherit it.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) shadowing an OR pharmacist on rotation if available; (2) reading published OR pharmacy case studies and the ASHP OR pharmacy guidelines; and (3) considering OR pharmacy as a niche specialization, since the field is small but growing and entry-level positions are increasingly available.

Today we covered OR pharmacy. Pharmacists embedded in surgical services improve antibiotic prophylaxis timing, drug shortage management, and controlled substance reconciliation. ASHP has published guidelines for OR pharmacy practice. I'll explore this niche on rotation.
OR pharmacy is an emerging specialty. Pharmacists work in surgery.

This session focused on neonatal intensive care safety — patients with extreme weight-based dosing requirements (some neonates are under one kilogram), immature drug metabolism that requires age-specific pharmacokinetic considerations, and rapid clinical change requiring frequent dose adjustments. I learned that NICU medication errors are concentrated in concentration confusion (dilutions that are correct for adult patients are often dangerous for neonates), miscalculated weight-based doses (where a small math error becomes a 10-fold dosing error), and decimal-point misplacement in orders or compounding. NICU-specific controls include standardized concentrations dispensed only in pediatric-appropriate strengths, computerized dose calculation that constrains prescribing to age-and-weight-validated ranges, mandatory pharmacist verification of all NICU orders, and patient-specific dispensing with no floor stock for HAMs. We also discussed the cross-disciplinary safety culture in NICU settings, which is typically among the strongest in any hospital because the consequences of error are visible and irreversible.

What this was important for is appreciating that NICU pharmacy is the most exacting weight-based-dosing environment in healthcare. The personal piece is awareness that this work requires both clinical depth and personal disposition — meticulous attention, comfort with high stakes, and the ability to function calmly when the patient population is at extremes of vulnerability.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) requesting a NICU shadowing experience even if a full rotation isn't available; (2) practicing pediatric and neonatal dose math regularly during my training; and (3) considering pediatric residency tracks and BPS Pediatric Pharmacy specialization as possible career directions.

Today's class covered NICU safety. Extreme weight-based dosing, immature drug metabolism, and decimal-point errors are core risks. Standardized concentrations, computerized dose calculation, mandatory pharmacist verification, and patient-specific dispensing are core controls. I'll request a NICU shadowing experience and practice pediatric dose math.
NICU is for premature and sick babies. Doses are weight-based and small errors are dangerous.

Today's class focused on patient safety in psychiatric settings — including overdose risk with controlled substances, drug-drug interactions in polypharmacy psychiatric regimens, clozapine ANC monitoring requirements, lithium toxicity at narrow therapeutic windows, and the behavioral safety considerations specific to psychiatric inpatient units. I learned that psychiatric medication safety has its own canon of controls: REMS-like clozapine programs that require ANC monitoring at defined intervals before dispensing can occur, lithium therapeutic drug monitoring with documented level review at every refill, restrictions on prescriber-dispensed medication samples in psychiatric practice, and structured discharge planning when patients have suicidal ideation history (since the discharge moment is one of the highest-risk periods). We also discussed the operational reality that psychiatric pharmacy is structurally different from general medical pharmacy — the emphasis on long-term medication management, the integration with mental health legal frameworks (involuntary commitment, court-ordered treatment), and the chronic-disease orientation of much psychiatric pharmacy work.

What this was important for is recognizing psychiatric pharmacy as having distinct safety considerations that don't reduce to generic medication safety principles.
