The personal piece is openness to psychiatric pharmacy as a potential focus area, since the field combines clinical complexity with measurable patient impact and is currently under-staffed relative to need.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing clozapine REMS in detail before any psychiatric rotation; (2) shadowing a psychiatric clinical pharmacist if my program offers the rotation; and (3) reading the BPS Psychiatric Pharmacy specialty content as a way of evaluating whether the field interests me as a career direction.

Today we covered psychiatric pharmacy safety. Clozapine ANC monitoring, lithium therapeutic drug monitoring, and structured discharge planning are core controls. The field has its own canon distinct from general medical pharmacy. I'll review clozapine REMS and consider psychiatric pharmacy as a focus.
Psychiatric drugs need careful monitoring. Clozapine and lithium have specific protocols.

Today's class focused on long-term care medication safety as a substantial pharmacy practice setting often invisible in pharmacy school curricula. I learned that nursing home residents are typically on 8 to 12 chronic medications, that consultant pharmacist medication reviews are federally required and structurally distinct from acute-care pharmacy, that the Beers Criteria and STOPP/START frameworks specifically flag medications that should be avoided or modified in older adults, and that the CMS Five-Star Quality Rating program for nursing homes includes pharmacy-related metrics that influence facility reimbursement and reputation. Consultant pharmacist services are reimbursed under specific Medicare and Medicaid pathways, and the role typically includes monthly medication regimen review, antipsychotic monitoring (since CMS has specifically targeted reduction of inappropriate antipsychotic use in nursing homes), polypharmacy review, and quarterly reporting to facility leadership. We also discussed the workforce and training pathways for long-term care pharmacy, including the BPS Geriatric Pharmacy specialty.

What this was important for is recognizing long-term care as a substantial clinical pharmacy practice setting with distinct safety considerations and a different rhythm from acute-care pharmacy. The personal piece is interest in this often-overlooked career path, since the field combines clinical depth with measurable population impact and offers schedule flexibility that acute-care pharmacy often does not.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading the AGS Beers Criteria thoroughly; (2) shadowing a consultant pharmacist if the experience is available; and (3) considering long-term care or geriatric pharmacy residency tracks.

Today's class covered long-term care pharmacy. Consultant pharmacist reviews are federally required and include monthly medication regimen review, antipsychotic monitoring, and polypharmacy review. The Beers Criteria and STOPP/START guide the work. CMS Five-Star Ratings include pharmacy metrics. I'll learn this area and consider it as a career path.
Nursing homes have consultant pharmacists. They review medications.

This session focused on home healthcare medication safety — including patients self-administering complex regimens after hospital discharge, home infusion services for IV antibiotics and chemotherapy, and the safety challenges of managing high-alert medications outside clinical supervision. I learned that home infusion pharmacy is its own discipline with NHIA (National Home Infusion Association) accreditation standards covering compounding workflows, training requirements for clinical staff, patient education materials, and emergency-response protocols. Home medication errors cluster around insulin self-administration (especially for newly initiated patients adjusting to dose-titration), anticoagulants (where the consequence of a missed or doubled dose is severe), and complex multi-drug regimens for chronic conditions (where polypharmacy increases the probability of skipped or duplicated doses). The class also covered the role of pharmacist-led home medication review programs in identifying and resolving regimen problems before they become harm events.

What this was important for is recognizing home healthcare as a growing pharmacy practice setting with substantial safety stakes that are systematically different from inpatient and traditional retail. The personal piece is interest in this expanding field, particularly home infusion pharmacy, which combines clinical depth with patient-relationship continuity in a way that other settings struggle to match.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) shadowing a home infusion pharmacy if available; (2) reading NHIA accreditation standards; and (3) considering home infusion as a possible practice area for early career.

Today's class covered home healthcare safety. Home infusion has NHIA accreditation standards. Common errors involve insulin self-administration, anticoagulants, and complex polypharmacy. Pharmacist-led home medication review reduces errors. I'll explore home infusion as a possible practice area.
Home healthcare has pharmacy services. There are safety risks at home.

This session focused on medication reconciliation across care transitions as the single highest-leverage patient safety intervention available to pharmacy. I learned that admission, intra-hospital transfer (medical floor to ICU or back), and discharge each represent reconciliation moments where errors cluster — and that pharmacist-led reconciliation reduces clinically significant medication discrepancies by roughly 50% in published trials, with corresponding reductions in adverse drug events at 30 and 90 days post-discharge. The Joint Commission requires medication reconciliation but the depth and quality vary widely across institutions; the difference between meaningful reconciliation and checkbox compliance is whether the reconciling clinician actually verifies the home medication list with the patient, the prior outpatient pharmacy, and the discharge summary, and whether the reconciliation is reviewed by a clinician with pharmacology depth. We discussed the canonical failure modes: missed home medications because the patient couldn't remember them, duplicated therapy because the new admission orders did not account for the home regimen, and discharge medications that conflict with the home regimen because the discharge writer did not have access to the verified home list.

What this was important for is recognizing reconciliation as core pharmacy practice rather than a documentation step. The personal piece is committing to depth in reconciliation work, not just compliance — a checkbox-completed reconciliation is functionally worthless if the underlying verification didn't happen.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) practicing structured medication histories at every inpatient rotation, including verification with the patient, the prior pharmacy, and prior records; (2) using interpreter services for non-English-speaking patients rather than relying on family translation, since translation errors are a known reconciliation failure mode; and (3) confirming high-risk reconciliations (anticoagulants, insulin, antiepileptics) directly with the prescribing service rather than relying on the chart.

Today we discussed medication reconciliation across transitions. Pharmacist-led reconciliation reduces clinically significant discrepancies by about 50% and reduces 30 and 90-day ADEs. The Joint Commission requires it but quality varies widely. Common failure modes include missed home medications, duplicated therapy, and conflicts between home and discharge regimens. I'll do thorough reconciliation on rotations.
Med rec is important at care transitions. Errors happen there.

Today's class examined chemotherapy as a system rather than as individual orders — protocols, order sets, verification workflows, dose-banding, multi-disciplinary review boards, and structured patient education. I learned that ASCO/ONS quality standards require that chemotherapy be administered only within a system that includes specific elements: regimen review by an oncology pharmacist or oncologist with regimen-specific training, dose calculation by an independent verifier, pharmacist-and-nurse double-checks before administration, structured patient education before and during the regimen, infusion monitoring with defined response protocols for hypersensitivity reactions, and side-effect management with pre-specified supportive care orders. Institutions that fail one element typically fail others, because the system elements depend on each other for execution. The systems framing explains why isolated error-prevention efforts (e.g., adding one more check, training one more person) don't substantially reduce harm — chemotherapy safety is a property of the system, not of individual diligence.

What this was important for is the systems framing of chemotherapy safety. The personal piece is recognizing that pharmacy contribution to chemotherapy safety requires whole-system competence rather than narrow technical skill.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing ASCO/ONS quality standards in detail; (2) requesting a comprehensive oncology rotation that exposes me to the full system; and (3) considering BPS Oncology Pharmacy specialization as a possible career direction.

Today's class covered chemotherapy safety as a system. ASCO/ONS standards specify the system elements: regimen review, independent dose calculation, double-checks, patient education, infusion monitoring, and side-effect management. Institutions failing one element typically fail others. I'll review the standards and consider oncology pharmacy.
Chemo needs a whole safety system to work right.

This session covered the Anesthesia Patient Safety Foundation (APSF) as a model for organized professional safety improvement. I learned that anesthesia mortality has fallen dramatically since the 1980s — from roughly 1 death per 10,000 anesthetics in the 1970s to roughly 1 in 200,000 today — through deliberate, organized professional safety work led by APSF and the broader anesthesia community. APSF's combination of professional advocacy, technology adoption (pulse oximetry as standard intraoperative monitoring, then capnography for verifying tracheal intubation), training redesign (CRM-style team training, simulation-based deliberate practice), and culture change has been one of healthcare's most successful safety transformations. We discussed why anesthesia succeeded where other specialties have struggled — a relatively narrow technical domain, strong professional society leadership, alignment of malpractice incentives with safety improvements, and a culture that accepted system-level interventions rather than relying primarily on individual clinician vigilance.

What this was important for is recognizing organized professional safety work as a viable pathway, and that pharmacy's safety challenges may be amenable to similar approaches. The personal piece is interest in advocacy roles and in understanding which safety improvements travel well from one specialty to another.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading the APSF history, especially the early years when the foundation was establishing professional norms; (2) following ASHP and ISMP advocacy work, which is the closest pharmacy analogue; and (3) considering professional safety leadership as a possible long-term career direction.

Today's class covered the Anesthesia Patient Safety Foundation. Anesthesia mortality dropped from 1 in 10,000 to 1 in 200,000 through organized professional safety work including pulse oximetry, capnography, CRM training, and culture change. The model is one of healthcare's safety success stories. I'll learn from this model.
Anesthesia got safer over time through organized work. There's a foundation for it.

Today's session focused on drug-drug interaction safety with a specific focus on clinical decision support alert fatigue. I learned that alert fatigue is now the central problem in DDI safety — the average prescriber receives many alerts per shift, the great majority of which are false positives or low-clinical-value, leading to a 'click-through' culture that desensitizes clinicians to high-value alerts when they appear. Studies have shown that override rates for DDI alerts often exceed 90% in mature EHR systems, and that the override is often automatic rather than reasoned. Tiered alert systems (where the highest-severity alerts cannot be overridden without explicit reason and additional sign-off), pharmacy-curated alert libraries (where pharmacy informatics teams suppress low-value alerts and elevate high-severity alerts), and 'top 50 high-severity interactions' lists are the leading interventions to restore alert credibility. The class also discussed the ongoing tension between safety and workflow efficiency in alert design — too few alerts and harm slips through, too many alerts and clinicians stop seeing them at all.

What this was important for is recognizing that DDI safety is now an alert-design problem more than a knowledge problem. The personal piece is interest in pharmacy informatics roles where this design lives.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing the institution's CDS DDI configuration during rotation; (2) suggesting low-value alerts to be suppressed when I observe them; and (3) developing my own short list of high-severity DDIs I always screen for manually rather than relying on the alert system.

Today's class covered DDI safety and alert fatigue. Override rates often exceed 90% in mature EHR systems. Tiered alerts, pharmacy-curated libraries, and high-severity lists are the leading interventions. I'll think about alert design and develop my own high-severity DDI list.
DDIs cause problems. Alerts can fatigue clinicians who click through them.

Today's class focused on allergy documentation as an under-recognized patient safety control. I learned that patient-reported drug allergies are frequently mislabeled — what the patient reports as an 'allergy' is often actually an intolerance (nausea, GI upset) or a non-allergic adverse reaction. The most consequential example is penicillin allergy: roughly 10% of patients carry a penicillin allergy label, but formal evaluation (skin testing, oral challenge) confirms true IgE-mediated allergy in less than 5% of those labeled. The 'penicillin allergy' label alone leads to broader-spectrum antibiotic use with worse outcomes — including higher rates of C. difficile infection, longer hospital stays, and higher mortality in published studies. Pharmacist-led allergy clarification (or formal allergy testing referral) restores penicillin eligibility for the majority of mislabeled patients and improves antibiotic stewardship. ISMP guidance includes structured allergy documentation requiring reaction description, time of onset, and severity rather than free-text 'allergy.'

What this was important for is recognizing allergy documentation as a clinical pharmacy intervention with stewardship and outcome implications, not as a clerical task. The personal piece is committing to clarify allergies routinely rather than accept the label as it appears in the EHR.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) clarifying every penicillin allergy I encounter with structured questioning during rotation; (2) referring to allergy clinic when appropriate for formal evaluation; and (3) updating EHR allergies based on clarification, including specific reaction description rather than the generic label.

Today's class covered allergy documentation. Most penicillin allergy labels are not true IgE-mediated allergies, and the label drives broader-spectrum antibiotic use with worse outcomes. Pharmacist-led clarification restores penicillin eligibility and improves stewardship. I'll clarify allergies on rotation.
Allergies need to be documented well. Most penicillin allergies aren't real.

Today's session focused on maternal and obstetric safety — including the alarming rise in U.S. maternal mortality (now the highest among high-income countries), Black maternal mortality disparities (Black women die at roughly 3 times the rate of white women in the U.S., a disparity that has been documented for decades and persists after adjusting for socioeconomic factors), and the role of medications in postpartum hemorrhage and hypertensive emergencies. I learned that AIM (Alliance for Innovation on Maternal Health) safety bundles include structured medication protocols for hemorrhage (oxytocin, methylergonovine, carboprost, misoprostol, tranexamic acid in defined sequence), hypertension (labetalol, hydralazine, nifedipine for severe hypertension; magnesium for seizure prophylaxis), and venous thromboembolism (postpartum prophylaxis for at-risk patients) that have produced measurable mortality reductions where implemented with fidelity. We also discussed the equity-specific aspects of maternal safety work — the structural drivers of disparity, the role of implicit bias in clinical decision-making, and the AIM Safe Health Care for Every Woman bundle that explicitly addresses equity.

What this was important for is recognizing maternal safety as both a high-stakes pharmacy area and a major equity issue, with structural rather than individual fixes carrying the most weight. The personal piece is awareness that pharmacy work in this area connects to systemic health justice in a way that's both motivating and demanding.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading the AIM bundle protocols; (2) shadowing an OB pharmacy team if available; and (3) bringing equity awareness to my work in any maternal-care setting.

Today's class covered maternal safety. U.S. maternal mortality is the highest among high-income countries with severe Black-white disparities. AIM bundles for hemorrhage, hypertension, and VTE reduce mortality. Equity is a major structural issue. I'll work on this and read the AIM protocols.
Maternal safety is important. There are racial disparities.

Today's final IPS session focused on geriatric patient safety — including polypharmacy (the average older adult takes 5+ chronic medications, and many take more), the AGS Beers Criteria for potentially inappropriate medications in older adults, the STOPP/START framework for systematic deprescribing and prescribing decisions, fall risk from CNS-active drugs, and the renal and hepatic dosing considerations that intensify with age. I learned that geriatric pharmacotherapy requires both deprescribing skill and careful initiation, since older adults are both more likely to be on inappropriate medications and more likely to be undertreated for evidence-based therapies (atrial fibrillation anticoagulation, statin therapy, osteoporosis treatment). The consultant-pharmacist model in long-term care provides one structural pathway for this work, but the same skills are needed in ambulatory geriatrics, transitions-of-care pharmacy, and primary care embedded pharmacy. We also discussed the BPS Geriatric Pharmacy specialty and the workforce shortage in this area as the U.S. population continues to age.

What this was important for is recognizing geriatric pharmacy as a high-need, high-leverage area as the U.S. population ages, with measurable mortality and quality-of-life impact from pharmacist intervention. The personal piece is interest in geriatric pharmacy practice as a career direction.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading the AGS Beers Criteria thoroughly; (2) practicing deprescribing conversations with my preceptor on rotation; and (3) considering geriatric pharmacy as a focus area for residency or early career.

Today's final session covered geriatric pharmacy safety. Polypharmacy, Beers Criteria, STOPP/START, fall risk from CNS drugs, and renal/hepatic dosing considerations are core elements. Older adults are both overprescribed inappropriate medications and underprescribed evidence-based therapies. I'll learn this area thoroughly and consider it as a career direction.
Older patients need careful prescribing. The Beers Criteria help.

Today's class focused on the FDA's Abbreviated New Drug Application (ANDA) process — the regulatory path for generic drug approval that requires demonstration of bioequivalence to the reference product rather than full clinical trial data. I learned that bioequivalence is established by demonstrating that the generic product produces drug exposure (area under the concentration-time curve, AUC, and maximum concentration, Cmax) within an 80-125% confidence-interval window of the reference brand product, with the testing typically conducted in healthy volunteers under fasted and fed conditions. The Hatch-Waxman Act of 1984 provides incentives for generic competition (180-day market exclusivity for the first generic challenger of a brand product) while preserving incentives for brand investment (patent protections, FDA exclusivity periods, pediatric exclusivity extensions). We also discussed Paragraph IV certifications — the mechanism by which generic manufacturers challenge brand patents as invalid or not infringed — and the litigation landscape that delays or accelerates generic entry, sometimes by years. The Orange Book is the FDA's published reference for approved drug products, including patent and exclusivity information that determines when generic entry is possible.

What this was important for is understanding that the prices my patients pay are partly determined by ANDA approval timing and the resulting generic competition, not just by drug-class clinical economics. The personal piece is realizing that being able to explain Hatch-Waxman dynamics to a patient asking why a generic is so expensive — or why no generic exists yet — is part of credible patient counseling.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading the Orange Book introduction so I understand its structure; (2) following one Paragraph IV case during my training to see how the litigation timeline works in practice; and (3) being prepared to explain generic-pricing dynamics in patient encounters when the question arises.

Today we covered the ANDA process for generic approval. Bioequivalence is the standard, demonstrated through 80-125% AUC and Cmax confidence intervals. Hatch-Waxman provides incentives for generic challenge through 180-day exclusivity for the first generic, and Paragraph IV certifications are the mechanism for challenging brand patents. The Orange Book lists approved products with patent and exclusivity information. I'll learn the Orange Book and follow Paragraph IV cases.
Generics are approved through the ANDA process. There are rules about bioequivalence.

Today's class covered biosimilar regulation under the Biologics Price Competition and Innovation Act (BPCIA), which created a regulatory pathway for biosimilars analogous to but distinct from the ANDA pathway for small-molecule generics. I learned that biosimilars are not generics — because biologics are produced in living systems (cell lines, microbial fermentation), biosimilars cannot be exactly identical to the reference product and must undergo a different approval pathway requiring analytical comparability, nonclinical comparability, and clinical comparability data, often with at least one comparative clinical trial. We discussed interchangeability — a higher regulatory bar than biosimilarity — that, once granted by FDA, allows pharmacist-level substitution under state laws (similar to small-molecule generic substitution) without prescriber notification. Naming conventions, including the four-letter suffix attached to biosimilar nonproprietary names (e.g., adalimumab-atto, infliximab-dyyb), are intended to support post-market pharmacovigilance and product-specific tracking. The FDA's Purple Book lists licensed biological products, including reference products, biosimilars, and interchangeable biosimilars.

What this was important for is understanding that biosimilar dispensing is regulated differently from generic dispensing and that pharmacist substitution authority depends on both interchangeability designation by FDA and on state law that authorizes substitution. The personal piece is committing to know my state's biosimilar substitution rules in detail before practicing, since the rules vary significantly across states and substitution without proper authority is a regulatory violation.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading the FDA's Purple Book and biosimilar policy documents; (2) verifying my state's biosimilar substitution law; and (3) practicing patient counseling specifically for biosimilar substitutions, which often differ from small-molecule generic substitutions in patient-experience and labeling considerations.

Today's class covered biosimilars under BPCIA. Biosimilars are not generics because biologics can't be exactly identical. Approval requires analytical, nonclinical, and clinical comparability. Interchangeability is a higher bar that allows pharmacist-level substitution under state laws. The four-letter naming suffix supports post-market tracking. I'll read the Purple Book and verify my state's substitution law.
Biosimilars are like generics but for biologics. They have different approval rules.

This session focused on orphan drug regulation under the Orphan Drug Act of 1983, which provides incentives for development of drugs treating rare diseases. I learned that the Act provides three primary incentives — seven years of market exclusivity from FDA approval (independent of patent protection), a 25% tax credit for clinical research costs, and FDA user fee waivers — for designation as an orphan drug, defined as a drug treating a disease affecting fewer than 200,000 U.S. patients. The Act has been credited with dramatic increases in rare-disease drug approvals over its 40-year history, with hundreds of products now approved that would not have been developed without the incentives. We also discussed the criticisms of the Act, particularly orphan-status designation gaming where common-disease drugs receive orphan status by being approved for narrow patient subgroups, and the resulting high prices that orphan exclusivity protects.

What this was important for is understanding why some drugs cost what they do — orphan exclusivity drives high prices in narrow markets, and patients with rare diseases often face severe affordability challenges even when their condition is treatable. The personal piece is being able to explain the orphan-pricing dynamic to patients confronted with sticker shock when they receive a rare-disease medication.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing the Orphan Drug Act provisions; (2) reading one published critique of orphan-pricing policies; and (3) being prepared to explain orphan economics in patient counseling encounters.

Today's class covered orphan drugs and the Orphan Drug Act of 1983. Seven-year exclusivity, tax credits, and user fee waivers incentivize rare-disease drug development. The Act has produced hundreds of approvals but has also been criticized for designation gaming and high prices. I'll learn the Act provisions and the pricing critique.
Orphan drugs are for rare diseases. They have special FDA rules.

Today's class covered the Combat Methamphetamine Epidemic Act (CMEA) and the federal pseudoephedrine sales restrictions it created in 2005. I learned that CMEA sets daily (3.6 grams) and 30-day (9 grams) sales limits per individual purchaser, requires logbook documentation including buyer name, address, signature, and government-issued ID for every sale, requires placement behind the counter or in a locked cabinet, and requires pharmacist or trained-technician completion of CMEA training. The Drug Enforcement Administration enforces these requirements through inspections and can sanction pharmacies that fail to maintain compliant logbooks or sales records. Some states impose more restrictive limits — Oregon and Mississippi have made certain pseudoephedrine products prescription-only, and other states have enacted lower daily or monthly limits than the federal floor. We discussed the operational reality that CMEA compliance is one of the most error-prone routine activities in retail pharmacy, with logbook gaps, missing buyer ID information, and incomplete training documentation among the most common DEA inspection findings.

What this was important for is recognizing CMEA as a federal law that retail pharmacists personally execute and personally face penalties for violating, including individual licensure consequences if compliance failures are systematic. The personal piece is committing to take CMEA compliance seriously in retail rotations, since the discipline of maintaining accurate logbooks every shift is what separates problem-free practice from DEA inspection findings.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing CMEA logbook requirements and the DEA's published guidance on PSE sales; (2) confirming my state's PSE rules and any restrictions tighter than federal; and (3) treating PSE sales as a compliance discipline rather than a routine task.

Today's session covered CMEA and pseudoephedrine restrictions. Daily limit of 3.6 grams, monthly limit of 9 grams, logbook documentation, behind-counter placement, and pharmacist training are required. Some states impose tighter limits including prescription-only PSE in Oregon and Mississippi. CMEA compliance is one of the most error-prone routine activities in retail pharmacy. I'll know my state's PSE rules.
Pseudoephedrine has rules to prevent meth production. There are sales limits.

Today's session focused on the Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act of 1990 (OBRA-90) and its state-level implementations, which together created the modern legal framework for pharmacist patient counseling. I learned that OBRA-90 created the federal mandate for pharmacist counseling of Medicaid patients, including specific requirements to offer counseling, document the offer or the patient's refusal, and conduct prospective drug utilization review (DUR) for issues like therapeutic duplication, drug-drug interactions, drug-disease contraindications, incorrect dosage or duration, and over-utilization or under-utilization. State laws in essentially every state have extended these federal requirements to all patients regardless of payer, making OBRA-90 the structural foundation of routine pharmacist counseling practice. The class walked through how the OBRA-90 requirements have been operationalized in retail workflow — the standard 'offer to counsel' that patients accept or decline at pickup, the technician's role in routing counseling-required prescriptions to pharmacist verification, and the documentation requirements that vary by state.

What this was important for is recognizing that the structure of retail pharmacy counseling we now treat as routine was statutorily created and has specific compliance requirements. The personal piece is understanding that the 'offer to counsel' is a regulatory floor, not a ceiling — meeting the federal and state minimum is not the same as providing substantive counseling, and the difference between checkbox compliance and actual patient education is what produces patient-outcome differences.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading my state's pharmacist counseling regulations including the specific topics required and the documentation expectations; (2) treating counseling offers as substantive opportunities rather than checkbox compliance; and (3) documenting both offers and substantive counseling content in the dispensing record so the documentation reflects actual practice.

Today we covered OBRA-90 and patient counseling requirements. The federal law mandates Medicaid counseling and prospective DUR. State laws extended this to all patients. The 'offer to counsel' is a regulatory floor rather than a ceiling — substantive counseling is what drives outcomes. I'll learn my state's specific rules and do substantive counseling.
Pharmacists have to offer counseling. There are state and federal laws about it.

Today's class focused on the regulatory framework for pharmacist immunization, which has expanded dramatically over the past two decades and continues to evolve. I learned that pharmacist immunization authority is granted by state statute and implementing regulation, that all 50 states now permit pharmacist-administered immunizations to varying age groups and vaccine sets, and that the specific age and vaccine eligibility varies significantly across states — some states permit pharmacist administration to children as young as 3, while others restrict it to adults 18 and older. The PREP Act (Public Readiness and Emergency Preparedness Act) provides federal liability protections during declared public health emergencies and was used during COVID-19 to authorize pharmacists to administer vaccines outside their state-licensed scope. We discussed the standing order pathway (where a state health officer issues a written standing order authorizing pharmacist administration of specific vaccines), the collaborative practice agreement pathway (where individual pharmacists enter agreements with prescribers), and full prescriptive authority (where pharmacists can independently order vaccines), and how these pathways differ across states.

What this was important for is recognizing immunization authority as a state-by-state regulatory landscape that I'll need to learn for any state I practice in, and that the operational details of how immunization happens (standing order, collaborative practice, prescriptive authority) shape what's actually possible in daily practice. The personal piece is appreciating immunization as one of the highest-public-health-impact services pharmacists offer and one whose scope continues to expand.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) verifying my state's immunization scope and any pending legislation that would expand it; (2) maintaining current ACIP immunization knowledge through CDC updates; and (3) considering specialty immunization training for travel medicine, which is a rapidly growing pharmacy practice area.

Today's class covered pharmacist immunization regulation. State laws vary in age and vaccine scope. PREP Act provides federal liability protection during declared emergencies. Standing orders, collaborative practice agreements, and prescriptive authority are the main pathways. I'll know my state's rules and maintain current ACIP knowledge.
Pharmacists give vaccines under state laws. The rules vary by state.

Today's class focused on the regulation of pharmacy benefit managers (PBMs) — the companies that administer prescription drug benefits for insurers, employers, and government programs. I learned that PBMs determine formulary tiers (which drugs are covered and at what cost-sharing level), negotiate manufacturer rebates that influence which products are preferred, set pharmacy reimbursement rates including Maximum Allowable Cost (MAC) pricing, and control mail-order versus retail dispensing channels through plan design. The regulatory framework is fragmented — federal law (Medicare Part D regulations, ERISA preemption for self-funded employer plans) and state law each apply to different aspects, and recent state legislation has begun reshaping the landscape with anti-clawback laws (preventing PBMs from recovering pharmacy reimbursement after the fact through 'direct and indirect remuneration' fees), transparency reporting laws, and fair pharmacy reimbursement laws (requiring PBMs to reimburse independent pharmacies at rates that cover their actual cost of dispensing). The class walked through how DIR fees, rebates, and spread pricing each affect pharmacy economics and patient out-of-pocket costs.

What this was important for is understanding that the financial environment my pharmacy operates in is largely shaped by PBM contracts and PBM-related legislation, not by the pharmacy directly. The personal piece is committing to follow PBM-related state legislation throughout my career, since the regulatory environment is changing rapidly and the consequences for pharmacy practice and patient access are significant.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading my state's PBM legislation; (2) understanding the basics of MAC pricing, DIR fees, and spread pricing; and (3) following NCPA's PBM advocacy work, which has been the central voice for independent pharmacy in PBM policy debates.

Today's class covered PBM regulation. PBMs control formularies, rebates, and reimbursement. Federal law (Part D, ERISA) and state law each apply. Recent state legislation includes anti-clawback, transparency, and fair reimbursement laws. DIR fees, rebates, and spread pricing affect pharmacy economics. I'll follow PBM legislation and learn the basic mechanisms.
PBMs are middlemen between insurers and pharmacies. They affect prices.

Today's class focused on personal drug importation — the legal status of patients bringing prescription drugs from other countries into the U.S. for personal use, primarily from Canada and Mexico but also from European countries and online international pharmacies. I learned that personal importation is technically illegal under federal law for most prescription drugs, but the FDA has historically exercised enforcement discretion for small quantities of drugs intended for personal use, particularly for drugs not available in the U.S. or for severe cost differentials. The 1996 FDA Personal Importation Policy provides guidance on this discretion, with exceptions including drugs for serious conditions where no acceptable U.S. alternative exists. Recent state legislation in Florida, Colorado, Maine, and several other states has explored state-sanctioned drug importation programs from Canada, with mixed federal response. The 2020 final rule allowed states to apply to HHS for approval of importation programs, but as of recent reporting only Florida's program has been approved and few states have moved to implementation.

What this was important for is being prepared to counsel patients who tell me they've bought drugs from Canada, Mexico, or international online pharmacies — the conversation needs to be honest about both legal status and product-quality risk, without becoming a moral lecture. The personal piece is committing to compassionate, informed counseling rather than reflexive judgment when patients import from financial necessity.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing FDA's personal importation guidance; (2) preparing a non-judgmental counseling approach for patients who disclose imported product use; and (3) following state importation legislation as the regulatory landscape evolves.

Today we covered personal drug importation. It's technically illegal under federal law but enforcement is discretionary. State programs in Florida and a few others are being developed. The 2020 final rule allowed state applications to HHS. I'll learn FDA guidance and prepare for patient conversations.
People sometimes import drugs from other countries because they're cheaper.

Today's class focused on off-label drug use and the regulation of off-label promotion. I learned that off-label prescribing — using an FDA-approved drug for an unapproved indication, dose, age group, or population — is legal medical practice and is common in pediatrics, oncology, psychiatry, and several other specialties where labeling has lagged behind clinical evidence. Physicians may prescribe off-label based on their clinical judgment, and pharmacists may dispense off-label prescriptions without ethical or legal concern when the off-label use is supported by evidence. However, manufacturer promotion of off-label uses is restricted under FDA labeling regulations, and pharmaceutical companies have paid billions of dollars in False Claims Act settlements for off-label promotion — Pfizer for Bextra and Geodon, GSK for Paxil, and several others. We discussed the evolving regulatory line between scientific exchange (which is permitted) and unlawful promotion (which is not), and how the courts have limited FDA's enforcement authority in recent decisions while preserving the underlying restriction.

What this was important for is recognizing that the regulatory line is on manufacturer promotion, not on prescriber practice, and that pharmacists are sometimes the people who notice off-label patterns at the prescription counter. The personal piece is committing to evidence-based counseling regardless of label status — if the off-label use is clinically appropriate, the counseling should be substantive rather than dismissive.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) using DailyMed and other resources to verify approved versus off-label indications; (2) being prepared to counsel patients receiving off-label therapy without alarming them about the label question; and (3) recognizing that off-label use is often appropriate but warrants careful patient discussion.

Today's class covered off-label use. Prescribing off-label is legal medical practice but manufacturer promotion is restricted. Major False Claims Act settlements have penalized off-label promotion. The line between scientific exchange and unlawful promotion is evolving. I'll know the difference and counsel evidence-based.
Drugs are sometimes used off-label. There are rules about advertising for it.

Today's session focused on the expansion of pharmacist scope of practice — including pharmacist prescribing authority, test-and-treat programs (e.g., influenza, strep, COVID-19, urinary tract infections), provider status legislation, and reimbursement parity. I learned that pharmacist provider status remains a federal advocacy priority because Medicare Part B does not formally recognize pharmacists as providers, which limits direct reimbursement for clinical services and constrains the financial sustainability of pharmacist clinical roles. State-level scope expansion has moved faster than federal — including hormonal contraception prescribing in many states, naloxone standing orders in all states, smoking cessation prescribing in many states, travel-related immunizations, and recently CDC-recommended PrEP and HRT prescribing in some states. The class walked through how scope expansion typically progresses: a state pharmacy association proposes legislation, allied health groups (often nursing or physician assistants) negotiate the boundaries, and the legislature passes an enabling statute followed by board of pharmacy rulemaking that fills in operational detail.

What this was important for is recognizing that the practice scope I will operate within is being actively shaped by current legislation. The personal piece is committing to advocate for scope expansion as part of professional citizenship, since the scope of pharmacy practice in 2030 will be largely determined by what pharmacists do in advocacy work over the next several years.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) tracking my state's scope-of-practice legislation; (2) joining a pharmacy association that advocates on scope issues; and (3) being credentialed for the broadest scope available where I practice.

Today's class covered pharmacist scope expansion. State-level changes include hormonal contraception, naloxone, smoking cessation, travel immunizations, and PrEP in some states. Federal provider status under Medicare Part B is still advocacy work. I'll follow scope legislation and join a pharmacy association.
Pharmacists are getting more authority over time. The laws change.

Today's class focused on the Vaccine Adverse Event Reporting System (VAERS) and the broader vaccine pharmacovigilance framework. I learned that VAERS is a passive surveillance system jointly operated by the FDA and CDC that accepts reports of any adverse event following immunization, regardless of whether causation is established. Pharmacists administering vaccines are required by federal law to report specific adverse events including those listed on the Vaccine Injury Table (anaphylaxis, encephalopathy, intussusception, and others), any event described in the manufacturer's package insert as contraindicating further doses, and any event suggesting a pattern that warrants further investigation. We also covered the Vaccine Safety Datalink (VSD) and v-safe — active surveillance systems that complement VAERS by addressing its limitations (passive systems under-report events systematically). VSD links vaccination records with healthcare utilization data across multiple integrated health systems, providing population-scale vaccine safety analyses that VAERS cannot.

What this was important for is recognizing pharmacist VAERS reporting as a regulatory obligation, not a discretionary professional courtesy, and as a structural contribution to the broader vaccine safety system. The personal piece is committing to file VAERS reports rather than letting suspected events go unreported.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing the VAERS reporting form before my retail rotation so the form is not new to me when I encounter a reportable event; (2) practicing identification of reportable events using CDC's published case studies; and (3) committing to file at least one VAERS report during training under preceptor supervision if a reportable situation arises.

Today's class covered VAERS reporting. It's a passive surveillance system jointly operated by FDA and CDC. Pharmacists administering vaccines have reporting obligations for events on the Vaccine Injury Table, in package inserts, or suggesting patterns. VSD and v-safe are active surveillance complements. I'll know how to file VAERS reports.
VAERS tracks vaccine adverse events. Pharmacists report to it.

Today's session focused on FDA medical device regulation as it intersects with pharmacy practice — including diabetes monitors and continuous glucose monitors (CGMs), insulin pumps, inhaler devices, autoinjectors (EpiPen and similar), and the increasing number of devices bundled with drugs as combination products. I learned that medical devices are classified by FDA into Class I (low risk, general controls only), Class II (moderate risk, special controls including premarket notification under 510(k)), and Class III (high risk, premarket approval through PMA process similar in regulatory rigor to drug NDAs). Pharmacist counseling on device-drug combinations requires familiarity with device-specific FDA labeling, training requirements (some devices require pharmacist training before dispensing), and adverse-event reporting through the MedWatch system. The class covered specific examples including the EpiPen autoinjector, where device-related issues have led to recalls and label updates that pharmacists must communicate to patients, and the rapidly expanding CGM market where device-specific patient education is part of the dispensing encounter.

What this was important for is recognizing that pharmacy practice is increasingly a device-counseling profession alongside its drug-dispensing core, and that competent device counseling requires technical fluency that drug-only counseling does not. The personal piece is committing to substantive device counseling, not just verbal handoff with a device pamphlet.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing major device classifications and FDA's device approval pathways; (2) practicing inhaler technique counseling with a placebo device on rotation; and (3) reporting suspected device problems through MedWatch when they arise.

Today's class covered medical device regulation. Devices are Class I, II, or III with corresponding regulatory rigor. Class III approval is through PMA similar to drug NDAs. Many drugs come with devices (inhalers, EpiPens, CGMs) that need patient counseling. Pharmacists report device problems through MedWatch. I'll learn device classifications and practice technique counseling.
Medical devices have FDA classes. Pharmacists counsel on device-drug combinations.

Today's class focused on the federal-state conflict around cannabis. I learned that cannabis remains a Schedule I controlled substance under the federal Controlled Substances Act, with no federally accepted medical use and the highest potential for abuse, while many states have established medical cannabis programs (38 states and DC at recent count) and several have legalized recreational use. This conflict has produced complex banking, employment, and healthcare implications: cannabis-related businesses have limited banking access because federal law prohibits banks from servicing them, federal employees cannot use cannabis even in legalized states, and healthcare providers in VA facilities cannot recommend or document cannabis use. Pharmacists in states with medical cannabis programs face unresolved questions: counseling content (since federal Schedule I status means no FDA-approved labeling), formal pharmacy training (which is not standardized), VA versus state-licensed pharmacy distinctions, and DEA implications for any pharmacist participation given that pharmacist DEA registration is federal. The DEA's recent proposal to reschedule cannabis to Schedule III could substantially change this landscape, but the proposal is not yet finalized.

What this was important for is understanding that I will likely encounter cannabis-related counseling questions regardless of where I practice, and that the answers depend on a federal-state conflict that is unstable. The personal piece is committing to follow this evolving area rather than avoiding it.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing my state's medical cannabis program; (2) following federal rescheduling discussions; and (3) being prepared to counsel patients on drug-cannabis interactions when relevant — particularly around CYP3A4 interactions, anticoagulant effects, and CNS depressant additive effects.

Today's class covered cannabis and the federal-state conflict. Cannabis is federally Schedule I but legal medically in 38 states and DC and recreationally in many. The conflict creates banking, employment, and healthcare implications. DEA's proposed rescheduling to Schedule III could change the landscape. I'll follow this and prepare to counsel on drug-cannabis interactions.
Cannabis laws are confusing because federal and state disagree. Pharmacists deal with patient questions.

Today's session focused on the regulatory framework around involuntary psychiatric medications — specifically Section 35-style civil commitment statutes (named for Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 35 but with analogous provisions in many other states) and broader civil commitment authority. I learned that civil commitment for substance use treatment exists in many states with substantial variation in scope, duration, and procedural protections. The regulatory framework typically requires documented medical necessity, time-limited commitment with judicial review at intervals, and procedural protections including right to counsel and right to appeal. Pharmacist involvement varies but typically includes medication procurement, administration documentation, and discharge planning, with the additional consideration that the patient lacks consent capacity for the duration of the commitment. The ethical dimensions — autonomy, beneficence, paternalism, the long history of psychiatric commitment as an instrument of social control — were treated explicitly in the class, including the pharmacist's professional obligation to advocate for least-restrictive treatment when consistent with the patient's medical needs.

What this was important for is recognizing that some pharmacy practice settings include patients without consent capacity, and that the regulatory framework protects (and constrains) that practice. The personal piece is openness to psychiatric and addiction practice while taking the ethics seriously rather than treating involuntary medication as routine.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing my state's civil commitment law; (2) reading the AMA Code of Medical Ethics on involuntary treatment; and (3) considering psychiatric pharmacy practice with informed eyes rather than reflexive avoidance.

Today's class covered involuntary psychiatric medication and civil commitment. State laws govern this with variation in scope, duration, and procedural protections. Pharmacist involvement includes procurement, documentation, and discharge planning. The ethical dimensions are significant. I'll learn this area and read the AMA Code of Medical Ethics.
Some patients get medication without consent under state laws.

Today's class focused on the regulatory framework specific to pediatric prescribing — including the Best Pharmaceuticals for Children Act (BPCA), the Pediatric Research Equity Act (PREA), pediatric exclusivity provisions, and state-level requirements for pediatric weight-based dosing verification. I learned that BPCA and PREA together create a structural requirement for pediatric drug studies — BPCA provides incentives (six months of additional exclusivity added to existing patent or exclusivity protections) for manufacturers who voluntarily conduct pediatric studies, and PREA requires pediatric studies as part of approval for drugs that are likely to be used in pediatric populations. The combined effect over the past two decades has been substantial pediatric labeling improvements, with many drugs now carrying age- and weight-specific dosing guidance that did not exist in the 1990s. The class also discussed remaining gaps — drugs widely used off-label in pediatrics where studies have not been required or completed, and the persistent challenge of ethics-board approval for pediatric clinical trials.

What this was important for is understanding why pediatric labeling improved so much in the 2000s and 2010s — it was regulatory work, not market self-correction. The personal piece is appreciating the policy infrastructure underneath pediatric clinical pharmacy and recognizing that further improvement will require continued regulatory attention.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading the BPCA and PREA summaries on FDA's website; (2) checking pediatric labeling carefully on rotation, especially for drugs first approved before BPCA/PREA; and (3) recognizing remaining gaps where pediatric data is still limited and the dosing decision is essentially a clinical extrapolation.

Today's session covered pediatric prescribing rules. BPCA provides incentives for voluntary pediatric studies and PREA requires them for drugs likely to be used in pediatrics. Pediatric exclusivity adds six months to existing protections. The combined effect produced substantial labeling improvements over two decades. I'll read the summaries and check pediatric labeling carefully.
There are rules to encourage pediatric drug studies. Labeling has improved.

This final PSR session focused on the regulation of foreign-trained pharmacists seeking U.S. licensure. I learned that the Foreign Pharmacy Graduate Examination Committee (FPGEC) certification, administered by the National Association of Boards of Pharmacy, is required before foreign-trained pharmacists can sit for the NAPLEX. The certification involves educational equivalency review of the foreign pharmacy degree, English proficiency examination (TOEFL or equivalent at specified scores), and the FPGEE knowledge examination covering U.S.-relevant pharmacy practice. State-level requirements then apply for internship hours (typically 1,500 hours), state-specific jurisprudence examination (MPJE), and licensure. We discussed the equity questions around the time and cost burden of this pathway — the FPGEC certification process typically takes 12-18 months and costs several thousand dollars before any state-level requirements begin — and how this affects pharmacist workforce diversity in the U.S.

What this was important for is recognizing that the U.S. pharmacist workforce includes foreign-trained colleagues whose path to licensure was substantially harder than the domestic one, and whose perspective on U.S. pharmacy practice can be uniquely valuable. The personal piece is appreciating my colleagues' work and being supportive of equity in licensure pathways throughout my career.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading FPGEC requirements as background knowledge; (2) being supportive in mentoring foreign-trained colleagues if I am in a position to; and (3) following NABP discussions of licensure pathways as the regulatory landscape evolves.

Today's class covered foreign pharmacist licensure. FPGEC certification through NABP is required before NAPLEX, including educational equivalency, English proficiency, and the FPGEE exam. State-level internship hours, MPJE, and licensure follow. The pathway is demanding in time and cost. I'll be aware of this and supportive of foreign-trained colleagues.
Foreign pharmacists have to take additional exams to practice in the U.S.

Today's class focused specifically on speaking up to authority — challenging a senior physician, a manager, or a long-tenured colleague when you observe something unsafe. I learned that this is the most-rehearsed-but-least-practiced of all safety culture skills, and that the gap between knowing you should speak up and actually speaking up is not a knowledge or motivation problem but the absence of pre-rehearsed scripts plus the institutional permission to use them. The TeamSTEPPS CUS framework ('I'm Concerned, I'm Uncomfortable, this is a Safety issue') gives the speaker prepared language at three escalating levels of concern. The two-challenge rule provides a structured way to escalate when an initial concern is dismissed: if the first challenge is not acted on, the speaker is required to challenge again, often with a stronger frame. The 'time-out' phrase ('I need to stop here for a minute') gives the speaker permission to pause an active workflow that feels unsafe. We discussed how authority responses to these challenges shape the next person's willingness to speak up — making leader behavior the single biggest variable in whether speak-up culture takes hold in a unit.

What this clarified is that speaking up is a designed behavior, not a personality trait. The personal piece is committing to use the prepared scripts even when uncomfortable, since the discomfort is a feature rather than a bug — the scripts work precisely because they are usable when one's natural instinct is to defer.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) memorizing CUS and the two-challenge rule verbatim so the language is immediately available rather than requiring me to invent something in the moment; (2) practicing them in low-stakes rotation moments so the discipline is built before I need it under pressure; and (3) committing to use the script the first time I observe something genuinely unsafe during my training, rather than letting the moment pass.

Today's class covered speaking up to authority. The CUS framework, two-challenge rule, and time-out phrase give pre-rehearsed scripts. Authority responses shape the next person's willingness to speak up. I'll memorize the scripts and practice them in low-stakes moments.
It's hard to speak up to senior people. There are tools and scripts for it.

Today's session introduced the pre-mortem — a structured exercise where, before a project or change is implemented, the team imagines that it has already failed catastrophically and works backward to identify what would have caused the failure. I learned that pre-mortems surface concerns that wouldn't otherwise be raised in standard project planning because the framing makes pessimism socially acceptable. In a normal planning meeting, raising a concern feels like opposing the project; in a pre-mortem, every concern is invited and the most pessimistic team members become the most useful contributors. We discussed pharmacy applications: pre-mortems before launching a new EHR feature, before rolling out a medication reconciliation workflow, before opening a new clinic, before launching a stewardship intervention. The exercise typically takes 30-60 minutes and has a defined structure — the facilitator describes the failure scenario, team members write individually for 10 minutes, then share, then aggregate the concerns into a risk-mitigation plan.

What this was important for is recognizing that the social design of safety conversations matters as much as their content. The personal piece is committing to lead pre-mortems when I'm in a position to do so, and to suggest them when I'm not.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading Gary Klein's pre-mortem essays and the related decision-making research; (2) requesting to lead a pre-mortem on a small project during my training; and (3) using the format on any major project I lead in my career.

Today's session introduced the pre-mortem exercise. Imagining failure before a project starts surfaces concerns that wouldn't otherwise be raised. The format takes 30-60 minutes with structured individual writing, sharing, and risk-mitigation aggregation. I'll use this on projects I lead.
Pre-mortems are about imagining failure to find risks. They're useful.

Today's class focused on the relationship between clinician burnout and patient safety. I learned that burnout — defined by Maslach as emotional exhaustion, depersonalization, and reduced personal accomplishment — is associated with measurably higher rates of medical errors, lower likelihood of error reporting, and more frequent self-reported reduced effort. The pharmacy workforce specifically has reported burnout rates of 50% or higher in recent surveys, with metrics-driven productivity environments (chain pharmacies with strict per-hour script targets, hospital pharmacies with verification-rate metrics) correlated with higher burnout. The class also discussed the Quadruple Aim framework that explicitly adds clinician wellbeing as a fourth coequal dimension alongside the original Triple Aim (improved patient experience, improved population health, reduced cost), and the structural interventions that have been studied: workload management, schedule predictability, peer support programs, and reducing low-value administrative tasks.

What this was important for is recognizing that pharmacy safety culture cannot be separated from pharmacy workforce wellbeing. The personal piece is taking my own future burnout risk seriously rather than treating it as a personality test or a moral failing.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading the Quadruple Aim framework and the major Maslach Burnout Inventory literature; (2) tracking my own burnout indicators during training using validated instruments; and (3) engaging with workplace wellbeing programs at any institution I join, treating them as professional infrastructure rather than personal weakness.

Today's class covered burnout and safety. Burnout (emotional exhaustion, depersonalization, reduced accomplishment) increases errors and reduces error reporting. Pharmacy has high burnout rates correlated with metrics-driven environments. The Quadruple Aim adds clinician wellbeing to the Triple Aim. I'll watch my own wellbeing during training.
Burnout is a problem in pharmacy. It affects safety. I'll watch out for it.

Today's session focused on how diversity and inclusion contribute to safety culture. I learned that homogeneous safety teams predictably miss perspectives and identify a narrower set of risks than diverse teams; this is documented in HRO research showing that demographic diversity, professional diversity, and cognitive diversity all contribute to error detection through different mechanisms. We discussed pharmacy-specific examples: bilingual pharmacists catching translation errors that monolingual teams would miss, technicians from different shifts identifying workflow problems that are invisible to single-shift staff, the under-representation of pharmacy management diversity that limits leadership perspective on equity-relevant safety issues, and the systematic underutilization of nursing and frontline-tech voices in formal safety committees that are dominated by physician and senior pharmacist perspectives. The class also covered the published evidence that diverse teams produce better decisions on complex problems while also generating more interpersonal friction in the short term — the friction is part of the mechanism by which the better decision emerges, not a defect to be smoothed away.

What this was important for is recognizing diversity as a substantive safety asset rather than as a separate equity initiative bolted onto safety work. The personal piece is committing to actively seek diverse perspectives when I lead teams, including the perspectives that produce uncomfortable initial conversations.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading the HRO diversity literature; (2) ensuring my team conversations actively seek different perspectives; and (3) supporting pharmacy DEI programs at any institution I join.

Today's class covered diversity and inclusion in safety teams. Diverse teams catch more risks through demographic, professional, and cognitive diversity. Examples include bilingual pharmacists catching translation errors and tech voices identifying shift-specific workflow problems. The friction in diverse teams is part of how better decisions emerge. I'll seek diverse perspectives when leading teams.
Diversity helps safety teams catch more problems.

Today's class focused on generational differences in safety culture — the different expectations Boomers, Gen-X, Millennials, and Gen-Z bring about hierarchy, communication, feedback, and the visibility of work. I learned that recent research suggests younger pharmacists are more likely to expect transparent feedback, peer-level dialogue with senior colleagues, and explicit psychological safety, while older colleagues may value implicit-norm-based communication where seniority signals authority and feedback flows primarily downward. Productive safety culture work requires the team to make its norms explicit so that intergenerational expectations align rather than collide silently. The class also discussed how the workforce mix is shifting — pharmacy is a profession with substantial recent generational turnover, and units that fail to manage this transition can lose institutional knowledge or experience cultural friction that erodes safety culture.

What this was important for is recognizing that safety culture friction is sometimes generational rather than ethical — what looks like a values disagreement is sometimes a norm-translation problem — and that explicit norm-setting helps. The personal piece is preparing to operate across generations in any team rather than assuming my generational defaults are universal.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) being curious about senior colleagues' implicit communication norms rather than assuming they should adopt mine; (2) being explicit about my own expectations rather than expecting telepathy; and (3) treating intergenerational friction as a norm-translation problem rather than a moral failing.

Today's session covered generational differences in safety culture. Different generations have different communication, hierarchy, and feedback expectations. Productive safety culture work requires explicit norm-setting. I'll be aware of this in mixed-generation teams.
Different generations work differently. This affects safety culture.

Today's class focused on how remote pharmacy work — telepharmacy, remote verification, work-from-home clinical reviews, and remote-first ambulatory pharmacy services — affects safety culture. I learned that remote workers report lower psychological safety in some surveys (because informal cues are reduced and serendipitous hallway conversations don't happen) and higher reported error rates from interruption-rich home environments. Mitigations that have been studied include scheduled video check-ins that maintain face-to-face contact, shared digital workspaces with visible activity (so colleagues can see who is doing what), explicit error-reporting workflows that don't depend on hallway encounters, and structured asynchronous communication that allows reflection rather than reactive responses. We also discussed the operational reality that remote pharmacy work is increasing across community, hospital, and clinical settings, and that the safety-culture interventions designed for in-person practice need redesign rather than transplantation to remote contexts.

What this was important for is recognizing that the safety-culture interventions designed for in-person practice need redesign for remote work, not just porting. The personal piece is preparing for remote work as a likely component of my career rather than treating it as an alternative to 'real' pharmacy work.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) following remote-pharmacy best-practice publications; (2) being deliberate about communication norms in any remote role I take; and (3) using video for substantive conversations rather than defaulting to text or chat.

Today's class covered remote work and safety culture. Remote workers face reduced informal cues and interruption-rich home environments. Mitigations include video check-ins, shared digital workspaces, and structured asynchronous communication. I'll be ready for remote work as part of my career.
Remote work has different safety challenges. I'll think about it.

Today's class examined safety culture metrics beyond the AHRQ Hospital Survey on Patient Safety Culture (HSOPSC). I learned that the Safety Attitudes Questionnaire (SAQ) is widely used and has somewhat different psychometric properties than HSOPSC, with stronger emphasis on teamwork climate and stress recognition. The Just Culture Assessment Tool (JCAT) measures specifically the Just Culture dimensions of safety culture rather than the broader concept. Qualitative tools — leadership walk-rounds with structured observation, focus groups with frontline staff, and 360-degree feedback for unit managers — provide complementary insight that surveys cannot capture. The class emphasized that triangulation across instruments and qualitative observation gives the most complete picture, and that institutions sometimes 'survey-shop' to find a metric that produces a flattering score, which is itself a safety culture signal worth attention.

What this was important for is recognizing that the choice of safety culture instrument is itself revealing — what an institution measures and how it measures it tells you what it actually cares about. The personal piece is committing to use multiple data sources rather than treating any single survey score as definitive.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reviewing SAQ, JCAT, and other instruments so I know the major options; (2) treating any single safety culture score skeptically; and (3) supplementing surveys with direct observation when I'm in a position to do so.

Today's class covered safety culture metrics beyond HSOPSC. SAQ emphasizes teamwork climate. JCAT measures Just Culture dimensions specifically. Qualitative tools complement surveys. Triangulation across instruments gives the fullest picture. I'll use multiple sources rather than relying on any single score.
There are different safety culture surveys. I'll learn the options.

Today's class focused on leadership walk-rounds as a structural safety culture practice. I learned that scheduled rounds where senior leaders visit clinical units to ask 'what worried you about safety this week?' have published evidence of increasing reporting and surfacing latent issues that don't reach formal reporting channels. The structure matters more than the rounds themselves — rounds that lecture or audit produce less learning than rounds that listen and document, rounds that close the loop with visible follow-up build trust faster than rounds that vanish into management process, and rounds led by leaders who model vulnerability ('I want to learn from you' rather than 'I want to inspect your work') generate substantively different conversations. Pharmacy-specific implementations include directors of pharmacy walking the dispensing floor with structured questions, central pharmacy leaders rounding in clinical units where pharmacy-related concerns might surface, and joint walk-rounds with nursing and medical leadership that surface cross-disciplinary safety issues.

What this was important for is recognizing walk-rounds as a learnable leadership practice with measurable impact, not as performative management. The personal piece is committing to lead walk-rounds when I'm eventually in a senior role, and to participate substantively when I'm in a unit being rounded on.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) shadowing one institutional walk-round if my rotation permits; (2) studying its structure rather than just observing; and (3) preparing to lead walk-rounds in future leadership roles.

Today's class covered leadership walk-rounds. Leaders ask structured questions about safety concerns. Listening rather than auditing, closing the loop visibly, and modeling vulnerability all matter for the rounds to be effective. I'll shadow walk-rounds and prepare to lead them later.
Walk-rounds are leaders walking units to ask about safety. They help.

Today's session compared safety culture in academic medical centers versus community hospitals. I learned that academic centers tend to have more formal safety infrastructure — dedicated patient safety committees, safety dashboards integrated into the EHR, full-time medication safety officers, and access to research-grade safety culture instruments — but face challenges with rotating residents and trainees whose engagement with longitudinal safety culture is necessarily limited. Community hospitals tend to have more stable workforces with longer tenure, but often less formal infrastructure and fewer dedicated safety roles. Each setting requires different safety culture work: academic centers need to make safety culture portable across the trainee turnover that defines their workforce, while community hospitals need to build formal infrastructure on top of the informal culture their stable workforces produce. Pharmacists who move between these settings need to recalibrate their expectations and approach.

What this was important for is recognizing that 'best practice' in safety culture is partly setting-dependent. The personal piece is being open to either practice setting and learning the safety culture norms of each rather than assuming one is inherently better.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) requesting rotations in both academic and community settings; (2) noting safety culture differences explicitly; and (3) being prepared to adapt my work to setting norms rather than imposing one setting's norms on the other.

Today's class compared academic and community safety culture. Academic centers have more formal infrastructure but trainee turnover. Community hospitals have stable workforces but less formal infrastructure. Each requires different work. I'll learn both settings.
Academic and community hospitals have different safety culture features.

Today's class focused on unit-level safety champions — frontline staff who hold designated peer-leader roles in safety culture — as a distributed leadership model. I learned that effective champion programs include explicit role descriptions (so the champion knows what is expected), dedicated time for champion work (so it isn't unfunded labor on top of regular duties), training in safety frameworks (Just Culture, RCA basics, safety reporting workflows), and structured connection to institutional safety leadership (so the champion's frontline observations actually reach decision-makers). Champions surface unit-specific safety issues that wouldn't reach leadership otherwise — workflow quirks, near-miss patterns, frontline frustrations with safety controls — and translate institutional safety priorities into shift-floor practice in ways that institutional communication alone cannot accomplish. We also discussed why champion programs sometimes fail: when the role is unfunded, when champions burn out, or when the connection to institutional leadership is weak.

What this was important for is seeing safety culture as a distributed leadership function, not solely a director-level activity. The personal piece is interest in being a safety champion early in my career as a developmental experience.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) volunteering for safety champion roles when offered; (2) reviewing champion-program structures at my rotation sites; and (3) considering safety leadership as a long-term career thread.

Today's class covered safety champions. Frontline peer leaders surface unit-specific issues. Effective programs include explicit role descriptions, dedicated time, training, and connection to institutional leadership. I want to volunteer for champion roles.
Safety champions are frontline peer leaders. They help with safety culture.

Today's session focused on the role of the institutional Patient Safety Officer (PSO) — typically a senior leader responsible for the institution's patient safety program, often reporting to the chief medical officer or directly to the CEO. I learned that PSO responsibilities include sentinel event response coordination, RCA oversight (or RCA-light alternatives like Apparent Cause Analysis), safety culture survey administration and interpretation, regulatory reporting to The Joint Commission and state authorities, and integration of safety work across departments. Some institutions have dedicated pharmacy safety officers reporting to the PSO, with explicit pharmacy-related scope; others embed pharmacy safety in general medication safety roles or in the pharmacy director's role directly. The career path to PSO varies — some are physicians, some are nurses, some are pharmacists, and some are non-clinical patient safety specialists with relevant credentials.

What this was important for is recognizing the PSO role as a possible long-term career trajectory for pharmacy leadership, and one that combines clinical depth with system-level impact in ways that few other roles match. The personal piece is interest in this trajectory as a possible direction.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) requesting an informational interview with a PSO during training if accessible; (2) reading the National Patient Safety Foundation materials and its successor organization's resources; and (3) considering safety leadership as a residency focus.

Today's class covered the Patient Safety Officer role. PSOs lead institutional safety programs across sentinel event response, RCA oversight, and culture surveys. The career path varies. I want to learn more and consider it.
PSOs are leaders for safety in hospitals.

Today's session examined how healthcare safety culture has borrowed from industrial safety — particularly aviation, nuclear power, and manufacturing — and which crossovers have worked best. I learned that key concepts including high-reliability organization theory (HRO), Crew Resource Management (CRM), root cause analysis (RCA), failure modes and effects analysis (FMEA), and many statistical process control techniques all originated in industrial settings before being adapted for healthcare.
