The crossover is not perfect — healthcare's variability across patients, the role of clinical judgment, and the irreducible uncertainty in many clinical situations make some industrial concepts awkward to apply directly — but the cross-pollination has been substantial and continues. Recent work explores additional industrial concepts ('safety differently' from Sidney Dekker, resilience engineering from Erik Hollnagel) for healthcare adoption.

What this was important for is appreciating that healthcare safety is part of a broader interdisciplinary safety field, and that staying current requires attention to non-healthcare sources. The personal piece is committing to read at least one non-healthcare safety publication per year so I'm exposed to ideas before they cross over.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) subscribing to a non-healthcare safety publication; (2) attending one cross-discipline safety conference if accessible; and (3) bringing industrial concepts into pharmacy safety conversations when relevant.

Today's class covered industrial safety crossover. HRO, CRM, RCA, and FMEA come from industry. The crossover is imperfect but useful. I'll read non-healthcare safety publications and bring industrial concepts into pharmacy conversations.
Healthcare borrows safety concepts from other industries.

Today's class focused on storytelling as a safety culture tool — the disciplined use of patient harm narratives, near-miss accounts, and clinician experiences to build shared understanding of why specific safety practices matter. I learned that data-driven safety presentations alone produce less behavioral change than data combined with concrete, anonymized stories of patient impact. The cognitive science behind this is well-established: stories are encoded and retrieved differently than data, they generate emotional engagement that pure data does not, and they make the human stakes of safety work concrete in ways that statistics struggle to do. The risk of storytelling poorly done is sensationalism (which generates short-term engagement but trains audiences to expect entertainment rather than learning) or shaming (which generates defensiveness and reduces future error reporting). Done well — anonymized, contextualized, framed for systemic learning rather than individual attribution — storytelling grounds abstract safety concepts in human experience and produces durable behavior change.

What this was important for is recognizing that safety culture work has both quantitative and narrative dimensions, and that the narrative dimension is often the more powerful behavior-change lever. The personal piece is committing to learn storytelling as a leadership skill.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) collecting permitted patient stories during my training that illustrate safety lessons; (2) using stories thoughtfully when I teach or lead; and (3) avoiding identifying details that could harm anyone involved.

Today's class covered storytelling in safety culture. Stories combined with data drive behavior change more than data alone. Done well, stories ground abstract safety concepts in human experience. Done poorly, they sensationalize or shame. I'll learn storytelling as a leadership skill.
Stories help with safety culture learning.

Today's class focused on trust as the foundation of safety culture — the basic willingness of frontline staff to believe that reporting errors, raising concerns, and admitting limitations will be received constructively rather than punitively. I learned that trust takes years to build and minutes to break, and that a single visible punitive response to an error report can erode reporting rates across an institution for months as staff reconfigure their assumptions about what is safe to disclose. Trust-building leadership behaviors include consistent application of Just Culture principles (so staff can predict the response to a given event type), transparent communication about institutional decisions (especially when decisions affect frontline workflow), visible follow-through on safety commitments (so staff see that reports lead to action rather than to filing), and acknowledgment of leader uncertainty (so staff don't feel they must perform certainty themselves). The class also discussed why trust erosion is so consequential — once trust is lost, every subsequent safety culture intervention becomes harder, because staff are reading every action through the lens of past breach.

What this was important for is recognizing that everything else in safety culture rests on trust, and that trust is built or eroded through specific leader behaviors that compound over time. The personal piece is committing to the kind of consistent, predictable conduct that builds trust over time, even when consistency is harder than the alternative.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) being predictable in my responses to colleagues' errors; (2) following through on commitments visibly; and (3) treating my own credibility as a long-term safety asset rather than a personality feature.

Today's session covered trust as the foundation of safety culture. Trust takes years to build and minutes to break. Consistent Just Culture application, transparent communication, visible follow-through, and acknowledgment of uncertainty all build trust. I'll work on consistent conduct.
Trust matters in safety culture. It takes time to build.

Today's session focused on safety culture during organizational change — mergers and acquisitions, EHR transitions, leadership turnover, COVID-19 surge response, and major workflow redesigns. I learned that change periods consistently show higher error rates, that staff turnover during change disrupts the informal safety knowledge that lives in long-tenured staff, and that the institutions that maintain safety during change typically over-invest in communication during the transition, double their safety huddles, and protect frontline time during the disruption rather than asking staff to do change-management work on top of their clinical responsibilities. We discussed why post-merger periods are particularly high-risk — different cultures collide, integration complexity overwhelms management bandwidth, and the natural attention to merger logistics crowds out attention to the clinical work that the merger is supposedly preserving — and how safety officers prepare specifically for organizational change with pre-change risk assessments, change-period staffing plans, and post-change safety culture re-measurement.

What this was important for is recognizing that change is a known safety risk requiring deliberate preparation, not an inevitable degradation that institutions just have to tolerate. The personal piece is being prepared to recognize and respond to safety degradation during change in my career.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) reading the change-management safety literature; (2) volunteering for change-focused safety committees; and (3) being attentive to safety signals during any organizational change I experience.

Today's class covered safety culture during change. Mergers, EHR transitions, leadership turnover, and surge response all raise error rates. Successful institutions over-invest in communication, double safety huddles, and protect frontline time. Post-merger periods are particularly high-risk. I'll be aware of change-related safety risks.
Change can hurt safety. I'll watch out for it.

Today's final SC session focused on recovery culture — the safety culture pattern that values near-miss reporting and recovery from error as much as it values error prevention. I learned that high-reliability organizations actively celebrate recoveries: the technician who catches a wrong-strength dispense before it leaves the counter is recognized publicly, the nurse who catches a programming error during the IDC is acknowledged in the unit huddle, and the system records the recovery as a near-miss with system-improvement implications. By contrast, institutions that punish or hide near-misses lose the highest-volume source of system learning available to them — for every harm event that reaches a patient, there are typically dozens of near-misses that did not, and those near-misses contain the same diagnostic information about system vulnerabilities without requiring patient harm to surface them. Recovery culture inverts the typical incentive: reporting a near-miss is rewarded behavior, not risk-taking. The class emphasized that recovery culture is the synthesis of everything else we've covered — Just Culture, psychological safety, transparency, leadership response, and metric design all show up in whether near-misses are celebrated or hidden.

What this was important for is recognizing that recovery culture is what ties together every other element of safety culture into a coherent operational practice. The personal piece is committing to celebrate my colleagues' recoveries publicly when I observe them, regardless of whether anyone else is doing it.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) acknowledging colleagues' recoveries when I observe them; (2) reporting my own near-misses through institutional channels; and (3) when I'm in a leadership role, building recovery-celebration into the team's regular rhythm.

Today's final session covered recovery culture. Near-miss reporting and recovery from error should be celebrated as much as prevention. HROs publicly acknowledge recoveries. Institutions that hide near-misses lose the highest-volume source of system learning. Recovery culture is the synthesis of Just Culture, psychological safety, transparency, leadership response, and metric design. I'll celebrate colleagues' recoveries.
Catching near-misses is good. Recovery culture rewards reporting them.
